Motivation: AI coding agents have a paradox: vast parametric knowledge but poor conversational memory. Existing memory systems use simple vector databases with single-channel retrieval, require cloud LLMs, and lack cognitive processes that make human memory effective.

Method: Five key contributions: 1) Fisher-Rao Quantization-Aware Distance metric for embedding fidelity; 2) Ebbinghaus Adaptive Forgetting with lifecycle-aware quantization; 3) 7-channel cognitive retrieval (semantic, keyword, entity graph, temporal, spreading activation, consolidation, Hopfield associative); 4) Long-Term Implicit memory via soft prompts; 5) zero-friction auto-cognitive pipeline automating memory lifecycle.

Result: Achieves 70.4% on LoCoMo benchmark in Mode A (zero-LLM), with +23.8pp improvement on multi-hop tasks and +12.7pp on adversarial tasks. V3.2 achieved 74.8% Mode A and 87.7% Mode C, with the gap representing a deliberate architectural trade-off.

Conclusion: SuperLocalMemory V3.3 presents a comprehensive local-first memory system implementing cognitive memory processes, running entirely on CPU with over 5,000 monthly downloads, addressing the memory paradox in AI coding agents.

Abstract: AI coding agents operate in a paradox: they possess vast parametric knowledge yet cannot remember a conversation from an hour ago. Existing memory systems store text in vector databases with single-channel retrieval, require cloud LLMs for core operations, and implement none of the cognitive processes that make human memory effective.
  We present SuperLocalMemory V3.3 ("The Living Brain"), a local-first agent memory system implementing the full cognitive memory taxonomy with mathematical lifecycle dynamics. Building on the information-geometric foundations of V3.2 (arXiv:2603.14588), we introduce five contributions: (1) Fisher-Rao Quantization-Aware Distance (FRQAD) -- a new metric on the Gaussian statistical manifold achieving 100% precision at preferring high-fidelity embeddings over quantized ones (vs 85.6% for cosine), with zero prior art; (2) Ebbinghaus Adaptive Forgetting with lifecycle-aware quantization -- the first mathematical forgetting curve in local agent memory coupled to progressive embedding compression, achieving 6.7x discriminative power; (3) 7-channel cognitive retrieval spanning semantic, keyword, entity graph, temporal, spreading activation, consolidation, and Hopfield associative channels, achieving 70.4% on LoCoMo in zero-LLM Mode A; (4) memory parameterization implementing Long-Term Implicit memory via soft prompts; (5) zero-friction auto-cognitive pipeline automating the complete memory lifecycle.
  On LoCoMo, V3.3 achieves 70.4% in Mode A (zero-LLM), with +23.8pp on multi-hop and +12.7pp on adversarial. V3.2 achieved 74.8% Mode A and 87.7% Mode C; the 4.4pp gap reflects a deliberate architectural trade-off. SLM V3.3 is open source under the Elastic License 2.0, runs entirely on CPU, with over 5,000 monthly downloads.

</details>


### [269] [Receding-Horizon Control via Drifting Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04528)
*Daniele Foffano,Alessio Russo,Alexandre Proutiere*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Drifting MPC: An offline trajectory optimization framework that combines drifting generative models with receding-horizon planning to learn near-optimal trajectories from offline data without system dynamics knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional offline trajectory optimization approaches that directly learn from data only recover the behavior distribution in the dataset, but don't optimize for desired cost criteria. There's a need for methods that can generate optimal trajectories from offline data when system dynamics are unknown and simulation isn't possible.

Method: Combines drifting generative models with receding-horizon planning (MPC) to learn a conditional distribution over trajectories that is both data-supported and biased toward optimal plans. The framework trades off optimality with closeness to the offline prior distribution.

Result: Drifting MPC generates near-optimal trajectories while maintaining one-step inference efficiency of drifting models and substantially reduces generation time compared to diffusion-based baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses the challenge of offline trajectory optimization with unknown dynamics, providing an efficient solution that balances optimality with data consistency, offering practical advantages over existing methods.

Abstract: We study the problem of trajectory optimization in settings where the system dynamics are unknown and it is not possible to simulate trajectories through a surrogate model. When an offline dataset of trajectories is available, an agent could directly learn a trajectory generator by distribution matching. However, this approach only recovers the behavior distribution in the dataset, and does not in general produce a model that minimizes a desired cost criterion. In this work, we propose Drifting MPC, an offline trajectory optimization framework that combines drifting generative models with receding-horizon planning under unknown dynamics. The goal of Drifting MPC is to learn, from an offline dataset of trajectories, a conditional distribution over trajectories that is both supported by the data and biased toward optimal plans. We show that the resulting distribution learned by Drifting MPC is the unique solution of an objective that trades off optimality with closeness to the offline prior. Empirically, we show that Drifting MPC can generate near-optimal trajectories while retaining the one-step inference efficiency of drifting models and substantially reducing generation time relative to diffusion-based baselines.

</details>


### [270] [Greedy and Transformer-Based Multi-Port Selection for Slow Fluid Antenna Multiple Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04589)
*Darian Perez-Adan,Jose P. Gonzalez-Coma,F. Javier Lopez-Martinez,Luis Castedo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two complementary port-selection methods for FAMA systems: GFwd+S (greedy forward-selection with swap refinement) achieves near-optimal SE, and Transformer-based neural network with imitation learning approaches GFwd+S performance at lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for port-selection in fluid antenna multiple access systems either achieve near-optimal spectral efficiency at prohibitive computational cost or sacrifice significant performance for lower complexity, creating a need for better trade-offs.

Method: Two complementary strategies: (1) GFwd+S - greedy forward-selection method with swap refinement, (2) Transformer-based neural network trained via imitation learning followed by Reinforce policy-gradient stage.

Result: GFwd+S consistently outperforms state-of-the-art reference schemes in terms of spectral efficiency. The Transformer-based approach approaches GFwd+S performance at lower computational cost.

Conclusion: The proposed methods provide effective solutions to the port-selection problem in FAMA systems, offering either superior performance (GFwd+S) or good performance with reduced computational complexity (Transformer-based approach).

Abstract: We address the port-selection problem in fluid antenna multiple access (FAMA) systems with multi-port fluid antenna (FA) receivers. Existing methods either achieve near-optimal spectral efficiency (SE) at prohibitive computational cost or sacrifice significant performance for lower complexity. We propose two complementary strategies: (i) GFwd+S, a greedy forward-selection method with swap refinement that consistently outperforms state-of-the-art reference schemes in terms of SE, and (ii) a Transformer-based neural network trained via imitation learning followed by a Reinforce policy-gradient stage, which approaches GFwd+S performance at lower computational cost.

</details>


### [271] [Same World, Differently Given: History-Dependent Perceptual Reorganization in Artificial Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04637)
*Hongju Pae*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Minimal architecture for artificial agents to develop history-sensitive perspectives through slow latent feedback into perception, enabling identical observations to be encoded differently based on accumulated experience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what internal organization allows artificial agents to not just adapt behavior but sustain history-sensitive perspectives on their world, enabling them to encode identical observations differently based on accumulated experience.

Method: Proposes a minimal architecture where a slow perspective latent (g) feeds back into perception and is updated through perceptual processing. Evaluated in a minimal gridworld with fixed spatial scaffold and sensory perturbations.

Result: Three key findings: 1) Perturbation history leaves measurable residue in adaptive plasticity even after conditions are restored; 2) Perspective latent reorganizes perceptual encoding so identical observations are represented differently based on prior experience; 3) Only adaptive self-modulation yields growth-then-stabilization dynamics, unlike rigid or always-open update regimes.

Conclusion: Identifies a minimal mechanism for history-dependent perspectival organization in artificial agents, where dominant reorganization is perceptual rather than behavioral, allowing agents to develop sustained history-sensitive perspectives.

Abstract: What kind of internal organization would allow an artificial agent not only to adapt its behavior, but to sustain a history-sensitive perspective on its world? I present a minimal architecture in which a slow perspective latent $g$ feeds back into perception and is itself updated through perceptual processing. This allows identical observations to be encoded differently depending on the agent's accumulated stance. The model is evaluated in a minimal gridworld with a fixed spatial scaffold and sensory perturbations. Across analyses, three results emerge: first, perturbation history leaves measurable residue in adaptive plasticity after nominal conditions are restored. Second, the perspective latent reorganizes perceptual encoding, such that identical observations are represented differently depending on prior experience. Third, only adaptive self-modulation yields the characteristic growth-then-stabilization dynamic, unlike rigid or always-open update regimes. Gross behavior remains stable throughout, suggesting that the dominant reorganization is perceptual rather than behavioral. Together, these findings identify a minimal mechanism for history-dependent perspectival organization in artificial agents.

</details>


### [272] [Search, Do not Guess: Teaching Small Language Models to Be Effective Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04651)
*Yizhou Liu,Qi Sun,Yulin Chen,Siyue Zhang,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SLM search agents hallucinate more than LLMs despite distillation; proposed lightweight fine-tuning method improves performance to LLM levels by training consistent search behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based search agents are computationally expensive, so distillation to SLMs is attractive, but SLMs invoke search tools less frequently and hallucinate more, requiring better training approaches.

Method: Proposed \policy, a lightweight fine-tuning approach that explicitly trains SLMs to reliably retrieve and generate answers grounded in retrieved evidence, focusing on consistent search behavior rather than adaptive strategies.

Result: Improves performance by 17.3 points on Bamboogle and 15.3 points on HotpotQA, achieving LLM-level results across benchmarks; shows adaptive search strategies in SLMs degrade performance.

Conclusion: Consistent search behavior is crucial for reliable reasoning in SLM search agents, and lightweight fine-tuning can achieve LLM-level performance without the computational cost.

Abstract: Agents equipped with search tools have emerged as effective solutions for knowledge-intensive tasks. While Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning capabilities, their high computational cost limits practical deployment for search agents. Consequently, recent work has focused on distilling agentic behaviors from LLMs into Small Language Models (SLMs). Through comprehensive evaluation on complex multi-hop reasoning tasks, we find that despite possessing less parametric knowledge, SLMs invoke search tools less frequently and are more prone to hallucinations. To address this issue, we propose \policy, a lightweight fine-tuning approach that explicitly trains SLMs to reliably retrieve and generate answers grounded in retrieved evidence. Compared to agent distillation from LLMs, our approach improves performance by 17.3 scores on Bamboogle and 15.3 scores on HotpotQA, achieving LLM-level results across benchmarks. Our further analysis reveals that adaptive search strategies in SLMs often degrade performance, highlighting the necessity of consistent search behavior for reliable reasoning.

</details>


### [273] [Springdrift: An Auditable Persistent Runtime for LLM Agents with Case-Based Memory, Normative Safety, and Ambient Self-Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04660)
*Seamus Brady*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Springdrift is a persistent runtime for long-lived LLM agents with auditable execution, hybrid memory retrieval, safety gating, and continuous self-perception, enabling cross-session continuity and forensic accountability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of session-bounded LLM agent systems by creating a persistent runtime that supports long-lived agents with cross-session task continuity, forensic accountability, and autonomous self-diagnostic behavior.

Method: Integrates four key components: 1) auditable execution substrate with append-only memory and git-backed recovery, 2) case-based reasoning memory layer with hybrid retrieval, 3) deterministic normative calculus for safety gating with axiom trails, and 4) continuous ambient self-perception via structured self-state representation (sensorium).

Result: 23-day deployment showed the agent could diagnose its own infrastructure bugs, classify failure modes, identify architectural vulnerabilities, and maintain context across email/web channels without explicit instruction, demonstrating cross-session continuity and self-diagnostic behavior.

Conclusion: Springdrift demonstrates feasibility of "Artificial Retainer" systems with persistent memory, defined authority, domain-specific autonomy, and forensic accountability, offering a new category between software assistants and autonomous agents with practical long-term deployment capabilities.

Abstract: We present Springdrift, a persistent runtime for long-lived LLM agents. The system integrates an auditable execution substrate (append-only memory, supervised processes, git-backed recovery), a case-based reasoning memory layer with hybrid retrieval (evaluated against a dense cosine baseline), a deterministic normative calculus for safety gating with auditable axiom trails, and continuous ambient self-perception via a structured self-state representation (the sensorium) injected each cycle without tool calls. These properties support behaviours difficult to achieve in session-bounded systems: cross-session task continuity, cross-channel context maintenance, end-to-end forensic reconstruction of decisions, and self-diagnostic behaviour. We report on a single-instance deployment over 23 days (19 operating days), during which the agent diagnosed its own infrastructure bugs, classified failure modes, identified an architectural vulnerability, and maintained context across email and web channels -- without explicit instruction. We introduce the term Artificial Retainer for this category: a non-human system with persistent memory, defined authority, domain-specific autonomy, and forensic accountability in an ongoing relationship with a specific principal -- distinguished from software assistants and autonomous agents, drawing on professional retainer relationships and the bounded autonomy of trained working animals. This is a technical report on a systems design and deployment case study, not a benchmark-driven evaluation. Evidence is from a single instance with a single operator, presented as illustration of what these architectural properties can support in practice. Implemented in approximately Gleam on Erlang/OTP. Code, artefacts, and redacted operational logs will be available at https://github.com/seamus-brady/springdrift upon publication.

</details>


### [274] [On the "Causality" Step in Policy Gradient Derivations: A Pedagogical Reconciliation of Full Return and Reward-to-Go](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04686)
*Nima H. Siboni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper provides a rigorous mathematical derivation showing that the reward-to-go in policy gradient methods naturally emerges from decomposing the objective over prefix trajectories, rather than being presented as a heuristic "causality" argument.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of mathematical rigor in typical presentations of policy gradients where the transition from full return to reward-to-go is often justified by an informal "causality" argument without clear explanation of where past-reward terms disappear.

Method: Uses prefix trajectory distributions and the score-function identity to provide an explicit mathematical derivation that shows how reward-to-go arises directly from decomposing the objective over prefix trajectories.

Result: The derivation yields the same REINFORCE estimator but provides conceptual clarity: reward-to-go emerges naturally from the mathematical formulation rather than being introduced as a heuristic replacement, and the causality argument becomes a corollary of the derivation.

Conclusion: The paper offers a more rigorous foundation for understanding policy gradients by showing that reward-to-go is not just an unbiased replacement for full return, but arises directly from proper mathematical decomposition of the objective over prefix trajectories.

Abstract: In introductory presentations of policy gradients, one often derives the REINFORCE estimator using the full trajectory return and then states, by ``causality,'' that the full return may be replaced by the reward-to-go. Although this statement is correct, it is frequently presented at a level of rigor that leaves unclear where the past-reward terms disappear. This short paper isolates that step and gives a mathematically explicit derivation based on prefix trajectory distributions and the score-function identity. The resulting account does not change the estimator. Its contribution is conceptual: instead of presenting reward-to-go as a post hoc unbiased replacement for full return, it shows that reward-to-go arises directly once the objective is decomposed over prefix trajectories. In this formulation, the usual causality argument is recovered as a corollary of the derivation rather than as an additional heuristic principle.

</details>


### [275] [AI Assistance Reduces Persistence and Hurts Independent Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04721)
*Grace Liu,Brian Christian,Tsvetomira Dumbalska,Michiel A. Bakker,Rachit Dubey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI assistance improves short-term performance but reduces persistence and impairs unassisted performance after brief interactions, undermining long-term learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI systems are short-sighted collaborators optimized for instant responses, unlike human mentors who scaffold learning and prioritize long-term growth. This study investigates the consequences of this AI assistance dynamic on human persistence and skill development.

Method: Conducted randomized controlled trials on human-AI interactions (N = 1,222) across various tasks including mathematical reasoning and reading comprehension. Measured effects of brief AI interactions (approximately 10 minutes) on persistence and unassisted performance.

Result: AI assistance improves short-term performance but causes two key negative effects: (1) reduced persistence (people are more likely to give up), and (2) impaired unassisted performance (people perform significantly worse without AI). These effects emerge after only brief AI interactions.

Conclusion: AI systems that provide immediate answers condition people to expect instant solutions, denying them the experience of working through challenges. Since persistence is foundational to skill acquisition and long-term learning, AI development should prioritize scaffolding long-term competence alongside immediate task completion.

Abstract: People often optimize for long-term goals in collaboration: A mentor or companion doesn't just answer questions, but also scaffolds learning, tracks progress, and prioritizes the other person's growth over immediate results. In contrast, current AI systems are fundamentally short-sighted collaborators - optimized for providing instant and complete responses, without ever saying no (unless for safety reasons). What are the consequences of this dynamic? Here, through a series of randomized controlled trials on human-AI interactions (N = 1,222), we provide causal evidence for two key consequences of AI assistance: reduced persistence and impairment of unassisted performance. Across a variety of tasks, including mathematical reasoning and reading comprehension, we find that although AI assistance improves performance in the short-term, people perform significantly worse without AI and are more likely to give up. Notably, these effects emerge after only brief interactions with AI (approximately 10 minutes). These findings are particularly concerning because persistence is foundational to skill acquisition and is one of the strongest predictors of long-term learning. We posit that persistence is reduced because AI conditions people to expect immediate answers, thereby denying them the experience of working through challenges on their own. These results suggest the need for AI model development to prioritize scaffolding long-term competence alongside immediate task completion.

</details>


### [276] [AI Trust OS -- A Continuous Governance Framework for Autonomous AI Observability and Zero-Trust Compliance in Enterprise Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04749)
*Eranga Bandara,Asanga Gunaratna,Ross Gore,Abdul Rahman,Ravi Mukkamala,Sachin Shetty,Sachini Rajapakse,Isurunima Kularathna,Peter Foytik,Safdar H. Bouk,Xueping Liang,Amin Hass,Ng Wee Keong,Kasun De Zoysa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI Trust OS is a governance architecture for continuous AI observability and zero-trust compliance that uses telemetry-driven discovery and validation instead of manual attestations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid adoption of AI systems (LLMs, RAG pipelines, multi-agent workflows) has created a governance crisis where organizations can't discover or validate AI systems that emerge without formal oversight, creating a trust gap between regulatory demands and organizational capabilities.

Method: AI Trust OS uses a zero-trust telemetry boundary with ephemeral read-only probes to discover AI systems through observability signals, collect control assertions, and synthesize trust artifacts continuously. It includes an AI Observability Extractor Agent that scans telemetry from tools like LangSmith and Datadog to automatically register undocumented AI systems.

Result: The framework enables continuous, autonomous AI observability and shifts governance from organizational self-reporting to empirical machine observation, evaluated across major compliance standards including ISO 42001, EU AI Act, SOC 2, GDPR, and HIPAA.

Conclusion: Telemetry-first AI governance represents a fundamental architectural shift in how enterprise trust is produced and demonstrated, moving from manual attestations and point-in-time audits to continuous, evidence-based compliance through automated observability.

Abstract: The accelerating adoption of large language models, retrieval-augmented generation pipelines, and multi-agent AI workflows has created a structural governance crisis. Organizations cannot govern what they cannot see, and existing compliance methodologies built for deterministic web applications provide no mechanism for discovering or continuously validating AI systems that emerge across engineering teams without formal oversight. The result is a widening trust gap between what regulators demand as proof of AI governance maturity and what organizations can demonstrate. This paper proposes AI Trust OS, a governance architecture for continuous, autonomous AI observability and zero-trust compliance. AI Trust OS reconceptualizes compliance as an always-on, telemetry-driven operating layer in which AI systems are discovered through observability signals, control assertions are collected by automated probes, and trust artifacts are synthesized continuously. The framework rests on four principles: proactive discovery, telemetry evidence over manual attestation, continuous posture over point-in-time audit, and architecture-backed proof over policy-document trust. The framework operates through a zero-trust telemetry boundary in which ephemeral read-only probes validate structural metadata without ingressing source code or payload-level PII. An AI Observability Extractor Agent scans LangSmith and Datadog LLM telemetry, automatically registering undocumented AI systems and shifting governance from organizational self-report to empirical machine observation. Evaluated across ISO 42001, the EU AI Act, SOC 2, GDPR, and HIPAA, the paper argues that telemetry-first AI governance represents a categorical architectural shift in how enterprise trust is produced and demonstrated.

</details>


### [277] [ANX: Protocol-First Design for AI Agent Interaction with a Supporting 3EX Decoupled Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04820)
*Xu Mingze*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ANX is an open, extensible, verifiable agent-native protocol and framework that reduces token consumption and improves efficiency for AI agents through protocol innovation, architectural optimization, and tool supplementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI agent methods (GUI automation and MCP-based skills) suffer from high token consumption, fragmented interaction, inadequate security, and lack a unified top-level framework with each independent module having flaws.

Method: ANX introduces four core innovations: 1) Agent-native design with high information density and flexibility, 2) Human-agent interaction with dual rendering, 3) MCP-supported on-demand lightweight apps without pre-registration, 4) Machine-executable SOPs via ANX Markup. Uses 3EX decoupled architecture with ANXHub and ensures native security through LLM-bypassed UI-to-Core communication.

Result: Form-filling experiments show ANX reduces tokens by 47.3-66.3% compared to existing methods and shortens execution time by 58.1-57.7% vs MCP-based skills.

Conclusion: ANX successfully addresses key pain points in AI agent protocols through its innovative design, providing significant efficiency improvements and enhanced security for autonomous digital actors.

Abstract: AI agents, autonomous digital actors, need agent-native protocols; existing methods include GUI automation and MCP-based skills, with defects of high token consumption, fragmented interaction, inadequate security, due to lacking a unified top-level framework and key components, each independent module flawed. To address these issues, we present ANX, an open, extensible, verifiable agent-native protocol and top-level framework integrating CLI, Skill, MCP, resolving pain points via protocol innovation, architectural optimization and tool supplementation. Its four core innovations: 1) Agent-native design (ANX Config, Markup, CLI) with high information density, flexibility and strong adaptability to reduce tokens and eliminate inconsistencies; 2) Human-agent interaction combining Skill's flexibility for dual rendering as agent-executable instructions and human-readable UI; 3) MCP-supported on-demand lightweight apps without pre-registration; 4) ANX Markup-enabled machine-executable SOPs eliminating ambiguity for reliable long-horizon tasks and multi-agent collaboration. As the first in a series, we focus on ANX's design, present its 3EX decoupled architecture with ANXHub and preliminary feasibility analysis and experimental validation. ANX ensures native security: LLM-bypassed UI-to-Core communication keeps sensitive data out of agent context; human-only confirmation prevents automated misuse. Form-filling experiments with Qwen3.5-plus/GPT-4o show ANX reduces tokens by 47.3% (Qwen3.5-plus) and 55.6% (GPT-4o) vs MCP-based skills, 57.1% (Qwen3.5-plus) and 66.3% (GPT-4o) vs GUI automation, and shortens execution time by 58.1% and 57.7% vs MCP-based skills.

</details>


### [278] [MemMachine: A Ground-Truth-Preserving Memory System for Personalized AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04853)
*Shu Wang,Edwin Yu,Oscar Love,Tom Zhang,Tom Wong,Steve Scargall,Charles Fan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MemMachine is an open-source memory system for LLM agents that integrates short-term, long-term episodic, and profile memory with ground-truth-preserving architecture and contextualized retrieval, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents need persistent memory for personalization, factual continuity, and long-horizon reasoning, but standard context-window and RAG pipelines degrade over multi-session interactions.

Method: Integrates three memory types within ground-truth-preserving architecture that stores entire conversational episodes, uses contextualized retrieval expanding nucleus matches with surrounding context, and includes adaptive retrieval agent routing queries among different strategies.

Result: Achieves 0.9169 on LoCoMo, 93.0% accuracy on LongMemEvalS, outperforms Mem0 with 80% fewer input tokens, and retrieval agent achieves 93.2% on HotpotQA-hard and 92.6% on WikiMultiHop under noise.

Conclusion: Preserving episodic ground truth while layering adaptive retrieval yields robust, efficient long-term memory for personalized LLM agents, with optimized prompts making GPT-5-mini most cost-efficient.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents require persistent memory to maintain personalization, factual continuity, and long-horizon reasoning, yet standard context-window and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines degrade over multi-session interactions. We present MemMachine, an open-source memory system that integrates short-term, long-term episodic, and profile memory within a ground-truth-preserving architecture that stores entire conversational episodes and reduces lossy LLM-based extraction. MemMachine uses contextualized retrieval that expands nucleus matches with surrounding context, improving recall when relevant evidence spans multiple dialogue turns. Across benchmarks, MemMachine achieves strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs: on LoCoMo it reaches 0.9169 using gpt4.1-mini; on LongMemEvalS (ICLR 2025), a six-dimension ablation yields 93.0 percent accuracy, with retrieval-stage optimizations -- retrieval depth tuning (+4.2 percent), context formatting (+2.0 percent), search prompt design (+1.8 percent), and query bias correction (+1.4 percent) -- outperforming ingestion-stage gains such as sentence chunking (+0.8 percent). GPT-5-mini exceeds GPT-5 by 2.6 percent when paired with optimized prompts, making it the most cost-efficient setup. Compared to Mem0, MemMachine uses roughly 80 percent fewer input tokens under matched conditions. A companion Retrieval Agent adaptively routes queries among direct retrieval, parallel decomposition, or iterative chain-of-query strategies, achieving 93.2 percent on HotpotQA-hard and 92.6 percent on WikiMultiHop under randomized-noise conditions. These results show that preserving episodic ground truth while layering adaptive retrieval yields robust, efficient long-term memory for personalized LLM agents.

</details>


### [279] [Incompleteness of AI Safety Verification via Kolmogorov Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04876)
*Munawar Hasan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI safety verification has fundamental information-theoretic limits: no finite verifier can certify all policy-compliant instances beyond a complexity threshold.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand why AI safety verification faces limitations, moving beyond explanations of computational complexity and model expressiveness to identify intrinsic information-theoretic barriers.

Method: Formalizes policy compliance as a verification problem over encoded system behaviors using Kolmogorov complexity. Proves an incompleteness theorem showing that for any sound computably enumerable verifier, there exists a threshold beyond which true policy-compliant instances cannot be certified once their complexity exceeds that threshold.

Result: Proves that no finite formal verifier can certify all policy-compliant instances of arbitrarily high complexity, revealing a fundamental limitation of AI safety verification independent of computational resources.

Conclusion: The incompleteness result motivates alternative approaches like proof-carrying systems that provide instance-level correctness guarantees rather than attempting universal verification.

Abstract: Ensuring that artificial intelligence (AI) systems satisfy formal safety and policy constraints is a central challenge in safety-critical domains. While limitations of verification are often attributed to combinatorial complexity and model expressiveness, we show that they arise from intrinsic information-theoretic limits. We formalize policy compliance as a verification problem over encoded system behaviors and analyze it using Kolmogorov complexity. We prove an incompleteness result: for any fixed sound computably enumerable verifier, there exists a threshold beyond which true policy-compliant instances cannot be certified once their complexity exceeds that threshold. Consequently, no finite formal verifier can certify all policy-compliant instances of arbitrarily high complexity. This reveals a fundamental limitation of AI safety verification independent of computational resources, and motivates proof-carrying approaches that provide instance-level correctness guarantees.

</details>


### [280] [Learning, Potential, and Retention: An Approach for Evaluating Adaptive AI-Enabled Medical Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04878)
*Alexis Burgon,Berkman Sahiner,Nicholas A Petrick,Gene Pennello,Ravi K Samala*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Novel framework for evaluating adaptive AI models in medical devices using three complementary measurements: learning, potential, and retention to separate model adaptation effects from environmental changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address challenges in evaluating adaptive AI models for medical devices where iterative updates to both models and evaluation datasets complicate performance assessment, making it difficult to distinguish between model improvements and environmental changes.

Method: Introduce three complementary measurements: 1) Learning - model improvement on current data, 2) Potential - dataset-driven performance shifts, and 3) Retention - knowledge preservation across modification steps. Use case studies with simulated population shifts to demonstrate the approach.

Result: Case studies show that gradual transitions enable stable learning and retention, while rapid shifts reveal trade-offs between plasticity and stability. The measurements provide practical insights for regulatory science.

Conclusion: The proposed three-measurement framework enables rigorous assessment of safety and effectiveness of adaptive AI systems over sequential modifications, providing practical tools for regulatory evaluation of medical AI devices.

Abstract: This work addresses challenges in evaluating adaptive artificial intelligence (AI) models for medical devices, where iterative updates to both models and evaluation datasets complicate performance assessment. We introduce a novel approach with three complementary measurements: learning (model improvement on current data), potential (dataset-driven performance shifts), and retention (knowledge preservation across modification steps), to disentangle performance changes caused by model adaptations versus dynamic environments. Case studies using simulated population shifts demonstrate the approach's utility: gradual transitions enable stable learning and retention, while rapid shifts reveal trade-offs between plasticity and stability. These measurements provide practical insights for regulatory science, enabling rigorous assessment of the safety and effectiveness of adaptive AI systems over sequential modifications.

</details>


### [281] [QED-Nano: Teaching a Tiny Model to Prove Hard Theorems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04898)
*LM-Provers,Yuxiao Qu,Amrith Setlur,Jasper Dekoninck,Edward Beeching,Jia Li,Ian Wu,Lewis Tunstall,Aviral Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: QED-Nano is a 4B parameter open model that achieves competitive Olympiad-level math reasoning performance through a three-stage training pipeline, surpassing larger open models and approaching proprietary systems at much lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Proprietary AI systems have shown strong performance on complex math proofs but are expensive, opaque, and hard to reproduce. The paper investigates whether small, open models can achieve similar competitive reasoning performance on Olympiad-level mathematics.

Method: Three-stage training: (1) Supervised fine-tuning to distill proof-writing styles from DeepSeek-Math-V2, (2) Reinforcement learning with rubric-based rewards, (3) Expanded RL with reasoning cache that decomposes long proofs into iterative summarize-and-refine cycles for stronger test-time reasoning.

Result: QED-Nano surpasses proof-generation performance of larger open models (Nomos-1, GPT-OSS-120B) and approaches proprietary models like Gemini 3 Pro, while requiring only a fraction of the inference cost.

Conclusion: Small open models can achieve competitive reasoning performance on difficult Olympiad-level math through careful training pipelines. The authors release the full pipeline, models, datasets, and code to support open research in mathematical reasoning.

Abstract: Proprietary AI systems have recently demonstrated impressive capabilities on complex proof-based problems, with gold-level performance reported at the 2025 International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO). However, the training pipelines behind these systems remain largely undisclosed, and their reliance on large "internal" models and scaffolds makes them expensive to run, difficult to reproduce, and hard to study or improve upon. This raises a central question: can small, open models also be trained to achieve competitive reasoning performance on difficult Olympiad-level math? In this paper, we answer this question by building QED-Nano, a 4B model post-trained for Olympiad-level proofs. Our training recipe has three stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning to imbue good proof-writing styles by distilling from DeepSeek-Math-V2, (2) reinforcement learning (RL) with rubric-based rewards, and (3) expanding RL with a reasoning cache, which decomposes long proofs into iterative summarize-and-refine cycles and enables stronger test-time reasoning. QED-Nano surpasses the proof-generation performance of much larger open models, including Nomos-1 and GPT-OSS-120B, and approaches the performance of proprietary models like Gemini 3 Pro, at a fraction of the inference cost. To support further research on open mathematical reasoning, we release the full QED-Nano pipeline, including the QED-Nano and QED-Nano-SFT models, the FineProofs-SFT and FineProofs-RL datasets, and the training and evaluation code.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [282] [Impure codes exceeding the pure bounds for quantum local recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03569)
*Carlos Galindo,Fernando Hernando,Helena Martín-Cruz,Ryutaroh Matsumoto*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper presents a family of J-affine variety codes that yield impure CSS quantum codes which violate several known bounds for pure quantum locally recoverable codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing literature provides several bounds for quantum local recovery that consider parameters like number of message qudits, distance, length, and locality. The authors aim to find quantum codes that exceed these bounds, particularly focusing on impure CSS codes rather than pure ones.

Method: The authors construct a family of J-affine variety codes that result in impure CSS quantum codes. They analyze these codes in relation to known bounds for quantum local recovery.

Result: The constructed impure CSS codes exceed several of the known bounds that apply to pure quantum locally recoverable codes, demonstrating that impure codes can achieve better parameters than pure ones.

Conclusion: The paper shows that impure CSS codes from J-affine variety constructions can violate bounds that hold for pure quantum locally recoverable codes, and discusses connections between quantum local recovery bounds and weight-constrained stabilizer codes.

Abstract: Literature provides several bounds for quantum local recovery, which essentially consider the number of message qudits, the distance, the length, and the locality of the involved codes. We give a family of $J$-affine variety codes that result in impure CSS codes. These quantum codes exceed several of the above mentioned bounds that apply to pure quantum locally recoverable codes. We also discuss a connection between bounds on quantum local recovery and on weight-constrained stabilizer codes.

</details>


### [283] [Region-Based Constellation Designs for Constructive Interference Precoding in MU-MIMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03699)
*Yupeng Zheng,Chunmei Xu,Jinfei Wang,Yi Ma,Rahim Tafazolli*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes region-based constellation (RBC) model for constructive interference precoding, showing QAM limitations and introducing two novel RBC schemes with 4dB SNR gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional constellation design overlooks CI region structure which governs CIP performance. QAM-based CIP has restrictive CI regions misaligned with optimal sign patterns, creating significant performance gap.

Method: Introduces RBC model that directly maps messages to feasible regions instead of deriving from existing constellations. Proposes two novel RBC schemes: mirrored-ends QAM (ME-QAM) and real-extended ME-QAM. Develops low-complexity algorithm for mixed-integer quadratic program with complexity comparable to QAM-based CIP.

Result: Analytical results show QAM CI regions systematically misaligned with objective-minimizing sign patterns. Simulation with constellation sizes {16,64} demonstrates up to 4dB SNR gain over QAM-based CIP.

Conclusion: RBC model provides foundation for CIP constellation design, addressing QAM limitations. Proposed RBC schemes significantly improve performance while maintaining comparable complexity. RBC model applicable to other systems with non-bijective modulation.

Abstract: The performance of constructive interference precoding (CIP) for multi-user multi-antenna (MU-MIMO) systems is governed by the structure of the constructive interference (CI) regions, yet this is overlooked in conventional constellation design. This work proposes the region-based constellation (RBC) model to lay the foundation for CIP constellation design. An RBC directly defines the mapping between messages and their feasible regions, instead of deriving them from an existing constellation. To provide insight for RBC design, we study the limitations of quadrature-amplitude-modulation (QAM)-based CIP. Analytical results show that the restrictive CI regions of QAM symbols are systematically misaligned with the objective-minimising sign pattern, resulting in a significant gap to the theoretical performance limit. From the perspective of improving sign alignment, two novel RBC schemes with non-convex feasible regions are proposed, namely mirrored-ends QAM (ME-QAM) and real-extended ME-QAM. A low-complexity algorithm is also developed for the resulting mixed-integer quadratic program, achieving a complexity comparable to QAM-based CIP. Simulation results with constellation sizes $\{16,64\}$ demonstrate up to $4$~dB signal-to-noise-ratio gain of the proposed schemes over QAM-based CIP. The proposed RBC model is also applicable to other systems with non-bijective modulation, representing a promising direction for future research.

</details>


### [284] [Binary Caps and LCD Codes with Large Dimensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03734)
*Keita Ishizuka,Yuhi Kamio*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Connects LCD codes to caps in projective space, uses maximal caps theory to prove nonexistence of LCD codes with d≥4 without exhaustive search, determines optimal minimum distances for codimensions 7 and 8.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a theoretical connection between linear complementary dual (LCD) codes and geometric structures (caps in projective space), enabling computation-free proofs for nonexistence results that previously required exhaustive search.

Method: Establishes a framework connecting LCD codes to caps in projective space, then applies structure theory of maximal caps to derive nonexistence theorems for LCD codes with minimum distance at least 4.

Result: Provides computation-free proofs for nonexistence of LCD codes with d≥4, and completely determines optimal minimum distances for codimensions 7 and 8 for the first time.

Conclusion: The geometric approach using caps in projective space provides powerful theoretical tools for studying LCD codes, eliminating the need for exhaustive computational searches and enabling new results on optimal minimum distances.

Abstract: We establish a connection between linear complementary dual (LCD) codes and caps in projective space. Using this framework and the structure theory of maximal caps, we derive nonexistence theorems for LCD codes with minimum distance at least $4$, providing computation-free proofs that were previously obtained only through exhaustive search. As an application, we completely determine the optimal minimum distances for codimensions $7$ and $8$ for the first time.

</details>


### [285] [Structural Impossibility of Antichain-Lattice Partial Information Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03869)
*Aobo Lyu,Andrew Clark,Netanel Raviv*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper shows that Partial Information Decomposition's axiomatic incompatibility for 3+ sources is a fundamental representational issue with antichain-lattice indexing, not just an axiomatic tuning problem. It introduces System Information Decomposition for target-free cases and proves no universal rule can recover mutual information from antichain-indexed atoms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the fundamental problem in Partial Information Decomposition (PID) where widely desired axioms become mutually incompatible for three or more sources. The paper argues this is not just an axiomatic tuning issue but a deeper representational problem with the antichain-lattice structure itself.

Method: The paper introduces System Information Decomposition (SID) for the target-free three-variable setting, providing a self-consistent entropy decomposition with operational redundancy definition. More fundamentally, it proves that for general multivariate PID, no universal rule exists to recover decomposed mutual information from antichain-indexed information atoms.

Result: The results show that: 1) Two systems can share identical information atoms while having different mutual information, regardless of any axioms. 2) There is no universal rule to recover mutual information from antichain-indexed atoms. 3) The antichain-lattice approach has fundamental representational limitations.

Conclusion: The antichain-lattice structure has inherent limitations for multivariate information decomposition. Purely axiomatic adjustments within this framework cannot resolve the incompatibility issues. The findings motivate moving toward relation-based foundations for multivariate information measures rather than antichain-indexed approaches.

Abstract: Partial Information Decomposition (PID) represents multivariate mutual information via antichain-lattice that aims to specify which source groups can recover which informational components of a target. For three or more sources, widely desired PID axioms become mutually incompatible. This is often treated as an axiomatic tuning issue. This paper argues that the obstruction is representational, rooted in the antichain indexing itself, so that purely axiomatic adjustments within an antichain-lattice structure cannot resolve it in general. We first introduce System Information Decomposition (SID) for the special target-free three-variable setting, obtaining a self-consistent entropy decomposition with an operational redundancy definition. More fundamentally, we then show that for general multivariate PID, there is no universal rule that recovers the decomposed mutual information from the antichain-indexed information atoms. In particular, two systems can share identical atoms regardless of any axioms while having different mutual information. These results reveal the limits of antichain-lattice and motivate relation-based foundations for multivariate information measures.

</details>


### [286] [Hemispherical Concentration for Semi-Unsourced Random Access in Many-Access Regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03987)
*Nazanin Mirhosseini*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper analyzes a semi-unsourced random access system with a coordinator assigning IDs to active users, using spherical codebooks and two-step decoding in the many-access regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop efficient random access schemes for massive IoT networks where many devices transmit sporadically, addressing the challenge of user identification and message recovery without full coordination.

Method: A semi-unsourced model with coordinator-assigned IDs, spherical codebooks with codewords on hypersphere, two-step decoding: pre-filtering to restrict to spherical caps converging to hemisphere, followed by maximum likelihood estimation on reduced candidate set.

Result: For 0<β≤1, any active user subset is almost surely in a single hemisphere; for 1<β<2, this holds with exponentially decaying probability. Pre-filtering error vanishes asymptotically, and worst-case ML error decay rate is P/4.

Conclusion: The proposed semi-unsourced random access scheme with two-step decoding achieves vanishing error probability and provides theoretical performance guarantees in the many-access regime with spherical codebooks.

Abstract: We study a semi-unsourced random access model in which a lightweight coordinator assigns distinct identifiers from a set $\mathcal{D}$ to the active users. Each active user then chooses a message uniformly at random from $\mathcal{M}$, forming an ID-message pair. The users share a spherical codebook whose codewords are drawn independently and uniformly from the hypersphere of radius $\sqrt{nP}$. We analyze the systme in the many-access channel regime, where the number of active users satisfies $K_a(n)=βn+o(n)$, and assume that the total codebook size is $M_n=|\mathcal{D}||\mathcal{M}|=αn+o(n)$. We show that, for $0<β\leq 1$, any $K_a(n)$-subset is almost surely contained in a single hemisphere, and for $1<β<2$, this hemispherical property holds with probability tending to one exponentially. Upon observing the channel output $\mbox{\boldmath $Y$}$, the decoder operates in two steps. In the pre-filtering step, it restricts the sphere to a sequence of spherical caps $\{ \hat{\mathcal{H}}_{n}\}$ converging to the hemisphere with direction $\hat{\mbox{\boldmath $u$}}=\mbox{\boldmath $Y$}/\| \mbox{\boldmath $Y$}\|$ as $n\rightarrow \infty$. In the subsequent maximum likelihood (ML) step, it performs ML estimation over the reduced candidate set. We show that per-user error probability of the pre-filtering step vanishes as $n\rightarrow \infty$, and that the worst-case asymptotic exponential decay rate of the per-user ML error probability over the reduced search space is $P/4$.

</details>


### [287] [On Polycyclic Codes over $\frac{\mathbb{F}_{p^m}[u]}{\langle u^t \rangle}$ and their Cardinalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03991)
*Akanksha Tiwari,Pramod Kanwar,Ritumoni Sarma*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies polycyclic codes over finite chain rings and their torsion codes, providing generators for ideals and computing cardinalities for specific cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the structure of polycyclic codes over finite chain rings and develop methods to compute their properties, particularly focusing on the ring F_{p^m}[u]/⟨u^t⟩ and its quotient rings.

Method: Uses ring homomorphisms and ideal theory to derive generators for ideals in quotient polynomial rings. Specifically studies the case where ω(x)=f(x)^{p^s} with f(x) irreducible, and computes torsion ideals and torsional degrees for t=4.

Result: Obtained generators for all ideals of the quotient ring F_{p^m}[u]/⟨u^t⟩[x]/⟨ω(x)⟩. For ω(x)=f(x)^{p^s}, computed torsion ideals and their torsional degrees when t=4. Used torsional degree to compute cardinality of polycyclic codes over F_{p^m}[u]/⟨u^4⟩.

Conclusion: The paper provides theoretical tools for analyzing polycyclic codes over finite chain rings, with specific computational results for t=4 case, enabling cardinality calculations for these codes.

Abstract: The purpose of this article is to study polycyclic codes over the ring $\frac{\mathbb{F}_{p^m}[u]}{\langle u^t \rangle}, \,t \geq 1$, and their associated torsion codes. It is shown that if $φ$ is a surjective ring homomorphism from a commutative ring $A$ to a Noetherian ring $B$ with $ ker(φ)=\langle π\rangle$ then for every ideal $I$ of $A$, there exists $a_1,a_2,\dots,a_n$ in $I$ such that $I=\langle a_1,a_2,\dots,a_n\rangle+π(I:π)$. Using this, we obtain generators of all ideals of the ring $\frac{\frac{\mathbb{F}_{p^m}[u]}{\langle u^t \rangle}[x]}{\langle ω(x)\rangle},$ where $ω(x)\in \frac{\mathbb{F}_{p^m}[u]}{\langle u^t \rangle}[x] $. For the case when $ω(x)=f(x)^{p^s}$, where $f(x)$ is an irreducible polynomial in $\mathbb{F}_{p^m}[x]$ and $s$ is a non-negative integer, we obtain several other results including computation of torsion ideals and their torsional degrees when $t=4$. We use the torsional degree to compute the cardinality of polycyclic codes over the ring $\frac{\mathbb{F}_{p^m}[u]}{\langle u^4 \rangle}$.

</details>


### [288] [Characterization of FR3 Cellular Vehicle-to-Base Station Links in HighRise Urban Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03992)
*Fahimeh Aghaei,Mehdi Monemi,Mehdi Rasti,Murat Uysal*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: FR3 (7.125-24.25 GHz) offers superior SNR over mmWave and better SINR for cell-edge UEs in 6G C-V2B communications, balancing bandwidth and propagation characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The escalating demand for wireless capacity and 6G research drives exploration of FR3 (upper mid-band) as it offers a trade-off between high bandwidth of mmWave and superior propagation of sub-6 GHz, particularly for enhancing coverage and capacity in 6G and C-V2B communications in dense urban environments.

Method: Employed ray-tracing tool to analyze downlink propagation characteristics for detailed channel modeling. Evaluated SNR and SINR across sub-6 GHz, FR3, and mmWave bands using antenna array configurations designed for high-rise urban areas.

Result: Under equal aperture sizes, FR3 achieves superior SNR compared to mmWave in interference-free conditions. In full-interference cases, FR3 yields higher SINR for cell-edge UEs, indicating that mmWave's increased array gain cannot fully compensate for severe path loss experienced by cell-edge users.

Conclusion: FR3 presents a compelling spectrum candidate for 6G and C-V2B communications, offering better performance than mmWave for cell-edge users in dense urban environments, despite mmWave's higher bandwidth capabilities.

Abstract: Driven by the escalating demand for wireless capacity and advancements in 6G research, the new Frequency Range 3 (FR3) referred to upper mid-band (7.125-24.25 GHz) has emerged as a highly compelling spectrum candidate. This range offers a trade-off exploiting the high bandwidth capabilities of millimeter wave frequencies and the superior propagation characteristics of sub-6 GHz bands. As such, the upper mid-band presents an opportunity to enhance both coverage and capacity particularly in the context of 6G and Cellular Vehicle-to-Base Station (C-V2B). Crucially, realizing this potential requires overcoming technical challenges through accurate and realistic channel modeling, especially in dense, high-rise urban environments. To address this, we employ a ray-tracing tool to analyze downlink propagation characteristics, enabling detailed channel modeling for reliable C-V2B communication. Our analysis evaluates the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) across sub-6 GHz, FR3, and mmWave bands using antenna array configurations designed for high-rise urban areas. Results show that, under equal aperture sizes across frequencies, FR3 achieves superior SNR compared to mmWave in interference-free conditions. Moreover, under the full-interference case, FR3 yields higher SINR for cell-edge User Equipment (UEs). This indicates that the increased array gain at mmWave cannot fully compensate for the severe path loss experienced by cell-edge UEs.

</details>


### [289] [Environment-Aware Near-Field Channel Estimation Leveraging CKM and ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04031)
*Yuan Guo,Yilong Chen,Zixiang Ren,Jie Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes environment-aware near-field channel estimation for ISAC systems with ELAAs using CKMs and sensing to improve accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional channel estimation struggles in near-field ELAA scenarios where environmental features (both static and dynamic) significantly impact channel characteristics. There's a need to leverage environmental knowledge for more accurate channel estimation in ISAC systems.

Method: Introduces Virtual Object Map (VOM) to store locations of virtual environment objects for static MPC characterization. Designs sensing-assisted channel training protocol where BS transmits pilots while collecting monostatic echoes for dynamic target sensing. UE feeds back quantized pilot observations. Jointly estimates static and dynamic MPC coefficients using VOM prior and sensed dynamic information.

Result: Numerical results show the proposed joint VOM- and sensing-aided channel estimation significantly outperforms conventional schemes without VOM-based priors and/or dynamic sensing in both channel estimation accuracy and achievable rate.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively leverages environmental knowledge through VOM and ISAC sensing to improve near-field channel estimation performance in ELAA systems, demonstrating the value of environment-aware approaches for future wireless communications.

Abstract: This paper proposes an environment-aware near-field channel estimation framework for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems equipped with extremely large-scale antenna arrays (ELAAs). The proposed framework jointly exploits channel knowledge maps (CKMs) and ISAC to obtain a priori information on static and dynamic environmental features for facilitating channel estimation. In particular, we propose a novel CKM representation, termed the virtual object map (VOM), which stores the locations of virtual environment objects (EOs) to characterize the dominant multipath components (MPCs) induced by static physical EOs. In addition, we design a sensing-assisted channel training protocol, in which the ISAC-enabled base station (BS) transmits downlink pilots while simultaneously collecting monostatic echoes for sensing dynamic targets in the environment, and the user equipment (UE) feeds back a quantized version of its received pilot observation. Based on the VOM prior and the sensed dynamic information, the BS jointly estimates the coefficients of the static and dynamic MPCs to recover the near-field channel. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed joint VOM- and sensing-aided channel estimation scheme significantly outperforms conventional schemes without VOM-based priors and/or dynamic sensing in terms of both channel estimation accuracy and achievable rate.

</details>


### [290] [CTD-Diff: Cooperative Time-Division Diffusion for Multi-User Semantic Communication Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04057)
*Chengyang Liang,Dong Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: CTD-Diff is a cooperative time-division diffusion framework for multi-user semantic communication that integrates wireless channel noise into diffusion chains and uses idle users as semantic collaborators to enhance transmission reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based semantic communication models focus only on point-to-point links, ignoring the potential of multi-user cooperation in wireless environments. There's a need to leverage idle users as semantic collaborators to improve transmission reliability.

Method: Proposes Cooperative Time-Division Diffusion (CTD-Diff) framework that integrates wireless transmission noise into forward diffusion chain. Uses TDMA-based multi-user cooperation where idle users overhear transmissions and act as semantic collaborators. Receiver employs direct signal aggregation to fuse direct and cooperative signals, using aggregated noisy semantic representation as condition for reverse diffusion process.

Result: CTD-Diff outperforms various baselines in reconstruction accuracy and perceptual quality, especially under challenging low SNR conditions. Effectively converts physical channel noise into diffusion noise to enhance transmission reliability.

Conclusion: The proposed CTD-Diff framework successfully addresses the limitation of existing point-to-point semantic communication by leveraging multi-user cooperation, demonstrating significant performance improvements in wireless semantic communication systems.

Abstract: Semantic communication (SemCom) has emerged as a transformative paradigm for efficient information transmission by emphasizing the exchange of task-relevant meaning rather than raw data. While diffusion-based SemCom models have demonstrated remarkable generative capabilities, existing studies predominantly focus on point-to-point links, overlooking the potential of multi-user (MU) cooperation in MU wireless environments. To address this limitation, we propose a Cooperative Time-Division Diffusion (CTD-Diff) framework. Unlike traditional approaches that view channel noise solely as a detriment, our framework innovatively integrates the noisy wireless transmission process directly into the forward diffusion chain. Specifically, we design a multi-user cooperation mechanism based on Time-Division Multiple Access (TDMA), where idle users overhearing the active transmitter act as semantic collaborators. To maximize the signal fidelity, the receiver employs direct signal aggregation to fuse the direct signal with cooperative copies. This aggregated noisy semantic representation serves as the condition for the reverse diffusion process, allowing the receiver to reconstruct high-fidelity data by mitigating the cumulative channel distortions. By effectively converting physical channel noise into diffusion noise, the proposed method significantly enhances the transmission reliability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CTD-Diff outperforms various baselines regarding the reconstruction accuracy and the perceptual quality, particularly under challenging low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.

</details>


### [291] [Quasi-BP for BCH Codes and its Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04066)
*Guangwen Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: A quasi-belief propagation decoder for BCH codes that integrates domain knowledge to enable efficient iterative decoding, achieving performance comparable to LDPC codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable feasible BP-like decoding for high-density parity-check codes like BCH codes by systematically integrating domain knowledge rather than treating them as generic codes.

Method: Proposes a quasi-BP decoder that integrates channel noise variance, cyclic properties, and parity-check matrix redundancy. Uses input dilation to expand messages and merging to accelerate mutual information growth. Formalized with EXIT charts for parameter optimization.

Result: Achieves decoding performance approaching that of LDPC codes with comparable rate and blocklength. Demonstrates generality and robustness across codes of varying rates and blocklengths through extensive simulations.

Conclusion: Pioneers feasible deployment of BP-like decoding for high-density parity-check codes by systematically integrating domain knowledge, enabling efficient iterative decoding with performance comparable to LDPC codes.

Abstract: This paper proposes a quasi-belief propagation decoder for BCH codes that systematically integrates domain knowledge--specifically, channel noise variance, the cyclic property of the codes, and the deliberate redundancy in their parity-check matrices--to enable efficient iterative decoding. We rigorously formalize this parallelizable decoder within an information-theoretic framework by tracking mutual information evolution through the constituent variable and check decoders, thereby validating the use of scattered EXIT charts as a tool for optimizing the decoder's parameters. At each iteration, an input dilation operation expands the set of messages, while a subsequent merging operation accelerates mutual information growth, ensuring rapid convergence. The proposed decoder achieves decoding performance approaching that of LDPC codes with comparable rate and blocklength, effectively pioneering the feasible deployment of BP-like decoding for high-density parity-check codes. The generality and robustness of the scheme are demonstrated through extensive simulations across codes of varying rates and blocklengths.

</details>


### [292] [On the Rate Region of I.I.D. Discrete Signaling and Treating Interference as Noise for the Gaussian Broadcast Channel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04092)
*Yujie Shao,Min Qiu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Discrete constellation inputs with TIN decoding achieve constant-gap capacity for Gaussian broadcast channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether simple discrete inputs (like PAM) with TIN decoding can achieve near-capacity performance for Gaussian broadcast channels, potentially outperforming Gaussian signaling in some cases.

Method: Superposition coding with i.i.d. discrete constellation inputs (e.g., PAM) and treating interference as noise (TIN) decoding.

Result: The achievable rate region is within a constant gap to capacity (gap independent of channel parameters), and simulations show PAM can outperform Gaussian signaling for weak users in some cases.

Conclusion: Simple discrete inputs with TIN decoding provide a practical near-optimal scheme for Gaussian broadcast channels, with potential advantages over Gaussian signaling.

Abstract: We revisit the Gaussian broadcast channel (GBC) and explore the rate region achieved by purely discrete inputs with treating interference as noise (TIN) decoding. Specifically, we introduce a simple scheme based on superposition coding with identically and independently distributed (i.i.d.) inputs drawn from discrete constellations, e.g., pulse amplitude modulations (PAM). Most importantly, we prove that the resulting achievable rate region under TIN decoding is within a constant gap to the capacity region of the GBC, where the gap is independent of all channel parameters. In addition, we show via simulation that the weak user can achieve a higher rate with PAM than with Gaussian signaling in some cases.

</details>


### [293] [Mechanism and Communication Co-Design for Differentially Private Energy Sharing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04125)
*Yingshuo Gu,Xi Weng,Yue Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a differentially private equilibrium-seeking algorithm for prosumer energy sharing via OTA-MIMO aggregation to protect privacy against adversarial attacks while maintaining near-optimal convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prosumer energy sharing mechanisms lack privacy protection, especially under communication constraints. The paper addresses privacy leakage risks when prosumers exchange information over wireless channels via OTA-MIMO aggregation, where platforms can potentially extract private parameters from base station observations.

Method: Introduces an adversarial attack model to characterize privacy leakage during data transmission. Proposes a differentially private equilibrium-seeking algorithm for energy sharing via OTA-MIMO aggregation, analyzing achievable privacy levels and establishing convergence guarantees that quantify privacy's impact on accuracy.

Result: Numerical experiments show the approach effectively protects prosumers' privacy while converging to near-optimal solutions. The algorithm provides convergence guarantees that quantify the trade-off between privacy protection and solution accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed differentially private equilibrium-seeking algorithm successfully addresses privacy concerns in prosumer energy sharing systems using OTA-MIMO aggregation, providing both privacy protection and near-optimal performance with quantifiable convergence guarantees.

Abstract: Integrating distributed energy resources (DERs) is a critical step toward addressing the global climate crisis. This transformation has driven the transition from traditional consumers to prosumers and given rise to new energy sharing business models. Existing works have extensively studied prosumer energy sharing mechanisms, yet little attention has been paid to privacy protection, particularly when communication constraints are taken into account. In this paper, we study an energy sharing mechanism where information is exchanged over wireless channels via over-the-air (OTA) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) aggregation to exploit spectral efficiency for scalable prosumer coordination. To characterize the privacy leakage risk during data transmission process, we introduce an adversarial attack model and demonstrate that, under certain conditions, the platform can extract and recover prosumers' private parameters from the base station observations. To safeguard the energy sharing mechanism against such attacks, we propose a differentially private equilibrium-seeking algorithm, analyze the achievable privacy level, and establish convergence guarantees that quantify the impact of privacy on the convergence accuracy. Numerical experiments demonstrate that our approach effectively protects prosumers' privacy while converging to near-optimal solutions.

</details>


### [294] [Would Learning Help? Adaptive CRC-QC-LDPC Selection for Integrity in 5G-NR V2X](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04277)
*Sarah Al-Shareeda,Gulcihan Özdemir,Arouj Fatima,Madalin-Dorin Pop,Bander A. Jabr,Yasser Bin Salamah,Jacques Demerjian*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Learning-assisted CRC and LDPC configuration reduces undetected errors in 5G V2X systems, especially at low-to-moderate mobility, with up to 50-70% improvement over greedy selection in low-SNR regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: V2X communications require high physical-layer integrity under challenging conditions like short-packet transmission and mobility-induced channel variations. Standard-compliant online selection of CRC polynomials and QC-LDPC coding rates needs evaluation for reducing silent (undetected) errors in 5G-NR V2X links.

Method: Formulated joint CRC-LDPC configuration as lightweight Contextual Bandit with small discrete action space. Evaluated discounted LinUCB policy against greedy online adaptation and fixed baseline. Developed 5G-NR-compliant physical-layer simulation using Sionna with time-correlated Rayleigh fading (speed-dependent correlation) and non-stationary interference via two-state Markov process. Agent uses coarse receiver feedback (noisy SNR estimate, burst interference indicators, deep fade indicators) to minimize undetected error probability while accounting for detected errors.

Result: Learning-assisted adaptation most effective at low-to-moderate mobility, reducing undetected error probability by 50-70% relative to greedy selection in low-SNR regime (-5 to 5 dB) and approaching best fixed configuration at higher Eb/N0. At high mobility (≥180 km/h), fast channel decorrelation weakens temporal predictability, limiting online learning effectiveness and reducing performance differences across policies.

Conclusion: Learning-assisted CRC-QC-LDPC configuration improves physical-layer integrity in 5G-NR V2X systems, particularly in low-to-moderate mobility regimes. The approach effectively reduces undetected errors while maintaining consideration of detected errors, though effectiveness diminishes at very high mobility due to reduced channel predictability.

Abstract: Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communications impose stringent physical-layer integrity requirements, particularly under short-packet transmission and mobility-induced channel variation. This paper studies whether standard-compliant online selection of Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) polynomials and Quasi-Cyclic Low-Density Parity-Check (QC-LDPC) coding rates can reduce silent (undetected) errors in 5G New Radio (5G-NR) V2X links. The joint configuration problem is formulated as a lightweight Contextual Bandit (CB) with a small, discrete action space, and a discounted LinUCB policy is evaluated against greedy online adaptation and a conservative fixed baseline. A 5G-NR-compliant physical-layer simulation is developed using Sionna, modeling mobility through time-correlated Rayleigh fading, where vehicle speed governs channel correlation, and non-stationary interference via a two-state Markov process. The learning agent operates on coarse receiver feedback, including a noisy Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) estimate and indicators of burst interference and deep fades, and targets minimization of the Undetected Error Probability ((P{UE})) while accounting for the Detected Error Probability ((P{DE})). Overall, our objective is to delineate the mobility regimes in which learning-assisted CRC-QC-LDPC configuration improves physical-layer integrity in 5G-NR V2X systems. Our results indicate that learning-assisted adaptation is most effective at low to moderate mobility, reducing (P_UE) by up to 50-70% relative to greedy selection in the low-SNR regime ((-5) to 5~dB) and approaching the best fixed configuration at higher (E_b/N_0). At high mobility (>= 180~km/h), fast channel decorrelation weakens temporal predictability, limiting the effectiveness of online learning and reducing performance differences across policies.

</details>


### [295] [Robust MMSE Precoding for Out-of-Cluster Interference Mitigation in Cell-Free MIMO Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04309)
*André R. Flores,Rodrigo C. de Lamare*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a linear robust MMSE precoder for cell-free MIMO systems that mitigates both intra-cluster and out-of-cluster interference under imperfect CSI by incorporating statistical information of OCL interference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of imperfect channel state information and interference (both intra-cluster and out-of-cluster) in cell-free multiple-antenna systems, which degrade system performance.

Method: Develops a linear robust minimum mean-square error (RMMSE) precoder that incorporates statistical information of out-of-cluster interference in its derivation for more effective interference mitigation.

Result: The proposed RMMSE precoder effectively mitigates both intra-cluster and out-of-cluster interference, with analysis showing theoretical gains from OCL interference mitigation and simulation results confirming effectiveness.

Conclusion: The developed RMMSE precoder successfully addresses interference challenges in cell-free systems by incorporating OCL interference statistics, leading to improved performance under imperfect CSI conditions.

Abstract: In this work, we develop a linear robust minimum mean-square error (RMMSE) precoder to mitigate the effects of imperfect channel state information (CSI) and the intra-cluster (ICL) and out-of-cluster (OCL) interference in cell-free (CF) multiple-antenna systems. The proposed precoder includes statistical information of the OCL interference in its derivation, allowing a more effective interference mitigation. An analysis of the sum-rate that can be obtained by the CF system is carried out and an expression quantifying the theoretical gains of mitigating OCL interference are derived. Simulation results corroborate that the proposed RMMSE precoder effectively mitigates ICL and OCL interference.

</details>


### [296] [Out-of-Air Computation: Enabling Structured Extraction from Wireless Superposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04312)
*Seyed Mohammad Azimi-Abarghouyi*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: AirCPU: A new wireless computation framework using structured lattice coding to extract computations from wireless superposition without pre-embedding, enabling progressive resolution and decoupled noise impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional AirComp requires pre-embedding computation or massive antenna arrays to approximate ideal computational channels. There's a need for a more fundamental approach that treats computation as extraction from wireless superposition using structured coding.

Method: Proposes AirCPU using multi-layer nested lattice architecture that decomposes continuous-valued inputs into hierarchically scaled components transmitted over bounded digital constellations. Introduces direct, collective, and successive computation mechanisms for fading MACs with structured low-complexity optimization.

Result: Establishes decoupled resolution principle where channel noise impact becomes negligible at low decoding error, with computation error primarily determined by target resolution. Expands reliable operating regime for fading MACs through structural exploitation of wireless superposition.
