Conclusion: AirCPU provides a joint source-channel coding foundation for wireless computation that avoids pre-embedding, handles continuous data directly, and enables progressive resolution with decoupled noise impact through structured lattice coding.

Abstract: Over-the-air computation (AirComp) has traditionally been built on the principle of pre-embedding computation into transmitted waveforms or on exploiting massive antenna arrays, often requiring the wireless multiple-access channel (MAC) to operate under conditions that approximate an ideal computational medium. This paper introduces a new computation framework, termed out-of-air computation (AirCPU), which establishes a joint source-channel coding foundation in which computation is not embedded before transmission but is instead extracted from the wireless superposition by exploiting structured coding. AirCPU operates directly on continuous-valued device data, avoiding the need for a separate source quantization stage, and employs a multi-layer nested lattice architecture that enables progressive resolution by decomposing each input into hierarchically scaled components, all transmitted over a common bounded digital constellation under a fixed power constraint. We formalize the notion of decoupled resolution, showing that in operating regimes where the decoding error probability is sufficiently small, the impact of channel noise and finite constellation constraints on distortion becomes negligible, and the resulting computation error is primarily determined by the target resolution set by the finest lattice. For fading MACs, we further introduce collective and successive computation mechanisms, in addition to the proposed direct computation, which exploit multiple decoded integer-coefficient functions and side-information functions as structural representations of the wireless superposition to significantly expand the reliable operating regime; in this context, we formulate and characterize the underlying reliability conditions and integer optimization problems, and develop a structured low-complexity two-group approximation to address them.

</details>


### [297] [Beyond-Diagonal RIS For Enhanced Secrecy and Sensing Gains in Secure ISAC Networks: An Optimization Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04480)
*Elmehdi Illi,Marwa Qaraqe*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a secure beyond-diagonal RIS-aided ISAC scheme for multi-user multi-target networks, achieving improved sensing performance and secrecy-sensing trade-off compared to diagonal RIS baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) needs energy/spectrum efficiency. RIS helps in non-LoS environments, and beyond-diagonal RIS (BD-RIS) offers more control degrees of freedom for better performance in secure ISAC scenarios with eavesdropping targets.

Method: Proposes BD-RIS-aided ISAC scheme for multi-user multi-target network with DFRC BS. Formulates optimization problem to maximize weighted sum of per-target reflected powers under secrecy and power constraints. Uses alternating optimization and Riemannian conjugate gradient approach to find local optima for BD-RIS scattering matrix, beamforming matrices, and artificial noise covariance.

Result: BD-RIS design shows notable sensing gains over diagonal RIS baseline and improved secrecy-sensing trade-off - can increase system secrecy without degrading per-target reflected power.

Conclusion: BD-RIS enables robust secure ISAC in non-LoS environments, outperforming conventional RIS with better sensing performance and maintaining secrecy without sacrificing sensing capability.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) has been receiving a notable interest as an energy- and spectrum-efficient enabler for simultaneous communication and sensing. Notably, reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) is among the key technologies enabling robust communication and sensing, particularly in environments without a line-of-sight (LoS). Recently, a new type of RIS, called beyond-diagonal RIS (BD-RIS), has drawn attention, offering additional degrees of freedom in controlling the propagation medium. In this paper, a novel secure BD-RIS-aided ISAC scheme is proposed and evaluated. The scheme is applicable to a multi-user multi-target ISAC network, where a dual-functional radar-communication (DFRC) base station (BS) simultaneously serves multiple downlink users and senses various targets that aim to eavesdrop on the legitimate signal transmitted to the users. The presence of a BD-RIS enables circumventing the absence of the LoS link and ensures secure transmission and sensing. To this end, an optimization problem is formulated aiming at maximizing a weighted sum of per-target reflected powers, subject to secrecy and transmit power constraints. Thus, by virtue of an alternating optimization (AO)- and Riemannian conjugate gradient-based approach, local optima for the BD-RIS scattering matrix, transmit signal beamforming matrices, and artificial noise covariance matrix are obtained. Numerical results highlight (i) the notable sensing gains of the BD-RIS-aided design with respect to its diagonal RIS (D-RIS)-based baseline and (ii) the improved secrecy-sensing trade-off, whereby the BD-RIS can ensure an increasing system secrecy without degrading the per-target reflected power.

</details>


### [298] [Linear Exact Repair in MDS Array Codes: A General Lower Bound and Its Attainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04519)
*Hai Liu,Huawei Wu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper establishes fundamental lower bounds for repair bandwidth and I/O in MDS array codes under linear exact repair, showing the bounds are unattainable for r≥3 but tight for r=2 using finite geometry constructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of repair bandwidth and repair I/O for MDS array codes under linear exact repair, particularly how small these metrics can be when field size, redundancy, and sub-packetization are fixed while code length varies.

Method: Develops a geometric approach using subspace intersections and projective point configurations from parity-check realizations. Uses projective counting arguments to derive lower bounds, and constructs optimal codes for r=2 using Desarguesian spreads from finite geometry.

Result: Establishes a general lower bound for all r≥2, proves it's unattainable for r≥3 with ℓ≥2, but shows it's tight for r=2 using finite geometry constructions that achieve the bound up to maximum possible length q^ℓ+1.

Conclusion: Identifies a uniform obstruction governing linear exact repair and demonstrates that for two-parity cases (r=2), this obstruction is tight and optimal codes exist, while for higher redundancy (r≥3), the bounds cannot be achieved.

Abstract: For an $(n,k,\ell)$ MDS array code over $\mathbb{F}_q$, how small can the repair bandwidth and repair I/O be under linear exact repair? We study this question in the regime where the field size $q$, the redundancy $r=n-k$, and the sub-packetization level $\ell$ are fixed, while the code length $n$ varies, and we develop a geometric approach to this setting. Our starting point is an intrinsic reformulation of linear exact repair for MDS array codes in terms of subspace intersections and, for repair I/O, the projective point configurations induced by a parity-check realization.
  This viewpoint yields a simple projective counting argument establishing the general lower bound $$β_{\mathrm{avg}},β_{\max},γ_{\mathrm{avg}},γ_{\max}\;\ge\;\ell(n-1)-\frac{q^{(r-1)\ell}-1}{q-1}$$ for linear exact repair of every $(n,k,\ell)$ MDS array code over $\mathbb{F}_q$ with redundancy $r=n-k\ge 2$. To our knowledge, this is the first lower bound of this form that applies to arbitrary redundancy $r\ge 2$ and sub-packetization level $\ell$. At first glance, the projective counting bound appears rather coarse and therefore unlikely to be attained. We prove that this intuition is correct whenever $r\ge 3$ and $\ell\ge 2$.
  For $r=2$, the picture changes completely. Using Desarguesian spreads from finite geometry, we construct MDS array codes that attain the bound over a broad interval of code lengths, up to the maximum possible length $q^{\ell}+1$, and do so simultaneously for both repair bandwidth and repair I/O. In the smallest nontrivial case $(r,\ell)=(2,2)$, we also prove a converse within the regular-spread model.
  Together, these results identify a uniform obstruction governing linear exact repair and show that, in the two-parity case, this obstruction is tight.

</details>


### [299] [Pinching Antenna Systems (PASS): Enabling Reconfigurable and Controllable Wireless Channels -- A Comprehensive Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04521)
*Elmehdi Illi,Marwa Qaraqe*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Comprehensive review of Pinching Antenna Systems (PASS) - a novel wireless channel-altering technique using long waveguides with movable radiating antennas to enhance 6G network performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 6G networks face challenges from spectrum scarcity and the need to support both data-rate-hungry and sensing-centric applications, requiring more spectrum-efficient techniques than traditional multi-antenna systems.

Method: PASS extends base station reach by connecting RF chains to long waveguides with pinched radiating antennas at custom positions, enabling dynamic channel tuning to overcome unfavorable conditions like NLOS and path loss.

Result: PASS provides significant enhancements in sensing, network coverage, data rate, and security against eavesdropping, with various schemes designed for different system objectives including secure transmission and energy efficiency.

Conclusion: PASS shows promising potential as a channel-altering technique for 6G networks, though with limitations that require further research on deployment and implementation challenges.

Abstract: The evolution of wireless networks is driving new paradigms for consideration in upcoming generations. To this end, the 6G anticipates the development of several data-rate-hungry applications, in addition to a forecast growth in sensing-centric applications. Such an evolution, however, is unbalanced on the other side by the accentuated scarcity of spectrum, which opens up urgent needs to develop spectrum-efficient communication and sensing techniques. Due to the inability of the traditional multi-antenna schemes to enhance a wireless channel quality, increasing interest has been paid to wireless channel-altering schemes, such as reconfigurable intelligent surfaces and movable antennas. Recently, a new technique in this category, called pinching antennas (PAs), was introduced and tested. PA systems (PASS) are based on extending the reach of a base station by connecting its radio-frequency chains to long waveguides, on which one or many radiating antennas are pinched at custom positions of interest. Thus, such a technique can provide a means of overcoming several unfavorable channel conditions, such as the absence of a line-of-sight and increased free-space path loss. Importantly, such a channel-tuning feature can provide notable enhancements in terms of sensing, network coverage, data rate, and resilience against eavesdropping. In this work, we provide a comprehensive review of research on PASS, designed to meet various system design objectives, such as network coverage and data rate, information-theoretically secure transmission, sensing, integrated sensing and communication, and energy efficiency. A categorization of the surveyed work is established by comparing the various PASS schemes presented. Several takeaways are illustrated on the proposed schemes' potential and limitations, along with several directions forward discussed, in terms of future deployment and implementation.

</details>


### [300] [Identification for Colored Gaussian Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04674)
*Mohammad Javad Salariseddigh*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes identification capacity of Gaussian channels with correlated noise and ISI, showing super-exponential codebook growth even with sub-linear ISI memory growth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to understand the fundamental limits of identification (rather than transmission) in Gaussian channels impaired by correlated noise and inter-symbol interference (ISI), which are common impairments in practical communication systems.

Method: The analysis uses deterministic encoding functions with peak power constraints and studies colored noise with polynomially bounded singular value spectrum. The key technique involves analyzing the Mahalanobis-distance decoder induced by colored Gaussian noise statistics.

Result: Even when ISI memory grows sub-linearly with codeword length (∼n^κ where κ∈[0,1/2)), the codebook size exhibits super-exponential growth (∼2^{(n log n)R}). The paper characterizes bounds on identification capacity parameterized by κ and μ.

Conclusion: The paper establishes that identification capacity in Gaussian channels with correlated noise and ISI can support super-exponential codebook growth under certain conditions, providing fundamental insights into the limits of identification systems with practical channel impairments.

Abstract: We study the identification capacity of discrete-time Gaussian channels impaired by correlated noise and inter-symbol interference (ISI). Our analysis is formulated for deterministic encoding functions subject to a peak power constraint and colored noise whose covariance matrix features a polynomially bounded singular value spectrum, i.e., $\sim [n^{-μ} , n^{μ/2}]$ where $n$ is the codeword length and $μ\in [0,1/2)$ is the spectrum rate. A central result establishes that, even when the ISI memory length grows sub-linearly with $n,$ i.e., $\sim n^κ$ where $κ\in [0,1/2)$ and $κ+ μ\in [0,1/2),$ the codebook size continues to exhibit super-exponential growth in $n$, i.e., $\sim 2^{(n \log n)R},$ with $R$ representing the associated coding rate. Moreover, by employing the well-known Mahalanobis-distance decoder induced by colored Gaussian noise statistics, we characterize bounds on the identification capacity, with the resulting bounds parameterized by $κ$ and $μ.$

</details>


### [301] [LRC codes over characteristic $2$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04678)
*Francisco Galluccio*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Completed construction of LRC codes in even characteristic, achieving codes with length ≈q⁴, dimension/distance order q⁴, and locality r=q-1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To complete the construction of Locally Recoverable Codes (LRC codes) from previous work [6] specifically for the case of even characteristic, addressing gaps in existing constructions.

Method: Presents a general construction method for linear LRC codes in even characteristic, with detailed study of specific cases q=4 and q=8 to demonstrate the approach.

Result: Achieved LRC codes with large parameters: length n ≈ q⁴, dimension and distance of order q⁴, and locality r = q-1. Successfully constructed codes for q=4 and q=8 cases.

Conclusion: Completed the LRC code construction for even characteristic, providing a general framework that yields codes with optimal parameters and demonstrated practical applications through specific examples.

Abstract: In this work the construction of LRC codes given in [6] is completed, in the case of even characteristic. A general construction is presented, that enables us to obtain linear LRC codes of large length $n \approx q^4$, dimension and distance of order $q^4$, and locality $r =q-1$. In addition, the cases $q = 4$ and $q=8$ are studied.

</details>


### [302] [Partially deterministic sampling for compressed sensing with denoising guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04802)
*Yaniv Plan,Matthew S. Scott,Ozgur Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Optimized sampling scheme for compressed sensing that combines random and deterministic row selection from unitary matrices, improving image reconstruction for both generative and sparse priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In compressed sensing, practitioners often need to sample certain crucial rows deterministically rather than purely randomly, but existing theory doesn't formally address this hybrid approach. There's a need for a rigorous framework that combines random and deterministic sampling.

Method: Derived an optimized sampling scheme using Bernoulli selectors that naturally combines random and deterministic row selection from unitary matrices. The method determines which rows should be sampled deterministically based on optimization criteria.

Result: The proposed sampling scheme provides measurable improvements in image compressed sensing for both generative and sparse priors compared to with-replacement and without-replacement sampling. Theoretical results show improved sample complexity bounds and novel denoising guarantees.

Conclusion: The optimized hybrid sampling scheme offers a rigorous framework for combining random and deterministic sampling in compressed sensing, providing both theoretical improvements and practical benefits in image reconstruction applications.

Abstract: We study compressed sensing when the sampling vectors are chosen from the rows of a unitary matrix. In the literature, these sampling vectors are typically chosen randomly; the use of randomness has enabled major empirical and theoretical advances in the field. However, in practice there are often certain crucial sampling vectors, in which case practitioners will depart from the theory and sample such rows deterministically. In this work, we derive an optimized sampling scheme for Bernoulli selectors which naturally combines random and deterministic selection of rows, thus rigorously deciding which rows should be sampled deterministically. This sampling scheme provides measurable improvements in image compressed sensing for both generative and sparse priors when compared to with-replacement and without-replacement sampling schemes, as we show with theoretical results and numerical experiments. Additionally, our theoretical guarantees feature improved sample complexity bounds compared to previous works, and novel denoising guarantees in this setting.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [303] [Surrogate Model-Based Near-Optimal Gain Selection for Approach-Angle-Constrained Two-Phase Pure Proportional Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03371)
*Abhigyan Roy,Shreeya Padte,Abel Viji George,Vivek A,Satadal Ghosh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neural network model learns optimal navigation gains for two-phase proportional navigation guidance to minimize guidance effort based on engagement geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two-phase PPN guidance allows desired approach angles but has multiple feasible trajectories; optimal gain selection can minimize guidance effort, but analytical solutions are intractable for arbitrary geometries.

Method: Develop neural network regression model to learn nonlinear mapping between optimal gains and engagement geometries, using numerical simulations to train the model as a surrogate for optimal gain manifold.

Result: NN-based architecture predicts optimal gains with high accuracy, achieving coefficient of determination close to 0.9, enabling computationally efficient near-optimal 2pPPN guidance.

Conclusion: Neural network provides effective surrogate model for optimal gain selection in 2pPPN guidance, overcoming analytical intractability while maintaining high prediction accuracy for practical implementation.

Abstract: In guidance literature, Pure Proportional Navigation (PPN) guidance is widely used for aerodynamically driven vehicles. A two-phase extension of PPN (2pPPN), which uses different navigation gains for an orientation phase and a final phase, has been presented to achieve any desired approach angle within an angular half-space. Recent studies show that the orientation phase can be realized through multiple feasible trajectories, creating an opportunity to select navigation gains that minimize overall guidance effort. This paper addresses the problem of near-optimal gain selection for given initial and desired terminal engagement geometries. Two optimization problems are considered: i) determination of the optimal orientation-phase gain for a specified final-phase gain, and ii) simultaneously determining the optimal gain pair for both phases that minimizes the total guidance effort. Determining the optimal gains analytically for arbitrary engagement geometries is intractable. Numerical simulations further reveal that these optimal gains vary smoothly with respect to the engagement conditions. Exploiting this property, a neural network (NN)-based regression model is developed in this paper to learn the nonlinear mapping between optimal gains and initial and desired terminal engagement geometries. The trained NN serves as a computationally efficient surrogate for generating the optimal gains manifold, enabling near-optimal realization of 2pPPN guidance. Numerical simulation studies demonstrate that the developed NN-based architecture predicts optimal gains with high accuracy, achieving very high (close to 0.9) value of coefficient of determination.

</details>


### [304] [Activity-Dependent Plasticity in Morphogenetically-Grown Recurrent Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03386)
*Sergii Medvid,Andrii Valenia,Mykola Glybovets*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Developmental neural networks with co-evolved plasticity show anti-Hebbian plasticity outperforms Hebbian, with plasticity shifting from fine-tuning to adaptation under non-stationarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether co-evolution can independently discover optimal plasticity patterns in developmental neural networks, and to characterize the role of plasticity in grown networks versus random topologies.

Method: Characterized Hebbian/anti-Hebbian plasticity across 50,000 morphogenetically grown recurrent controllers (5M+ configurations on CartPole and Acrobot), then co-evolved plasticity parameters alongside developmental architecture. Compared with random-RNN control with matched topology statistics.

Result: Anti-Hebbian plasticity significantly outperforms Hebbian (Cohen's d = 0.53-0.64), regret reaches 52-100%, plasticity shifts from fine-tuning to adaptation under non-stationarity. Co-evolution independently discovers these patterns: 70% of runs evolve anti-Hebbian plasticity on CartPole. Morphogenetically grown networks show 2-6x higher regret than random graphs.

Conclusion: Co-evolution independently recovers optimal plasticity patterns, anti-Hebbian plasticity is superior for small recurrent networks, and developmental growth creates topology-dependent plasticity effects not found in random networks.

Abstract: Developmental approaches to neural architecture search grow functional networks from compact genomes through self-organisation, but the resulting networks operate with fixed post-growth weights. We characterise Hebbian and anti-Hebbian plasticity across 50,000 morphogenetically grown recurrent controllers (5M+ configurations on CartPole and Acrobot), then test whether co-evolutionary experiments -- where plasticity parameters are encoded in the genome and evolved alongside the developmental architecture -- recover these patterns independently. Our characterisation reveals that (1) anti-Hebbian plasticity significantly outperforms Hebbian for competent networks (Cohen's d = 0.53-0.64), (2) regret (fraction of oracle improvement lost under the best fixed setting) reaches 52-100%, and (3) plasticity's role shifts from fine-tuning to genuine adaptation under non-stationarity. Co-evolution independently discovers these patterns: on CartPole, 70% of runs evolve anti-Hebbian plasticity (p = 0.043); on Acrobot, evolution finds near-zero eta with mixed signs -- exactly matching the characterisation. A random-RNN control shows that anti-Hebbian dominance is generic to small recurrent networks, but the degree of topology-dependence is developmental-specific: regret is 2-6x higher for morphogenetically grown networks than for random graphs with matched topology statistics.

</details>


### [305] [Learning-Based Fault Detection for Legged Robots in Remote Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03397)
*Abriana Stewart-Height,Seema Jahagirdar,Nikolai Matni*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Off-line learning method detects single limb faults in quadruped robots using proprioceptive sensor data to enable automatic gait switching to tripedal locomotion for survival in hazardous environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadruped robots operating in hazardous environments face high risks of limb damage but lack natural ability to detect and adapt to such faults, unlike humans and animals. This capability is critical for survival in remote, dynamic settings where robots must autonomously complete assignments despite physical damage.

Method: Off-line learning-based approach using proprioceptive sensor data to detect single limb faults in quadruped robots. The fault detection system provides output to the controller to select appropriate tripedal gait based on current physical morphology.

Result: Development and implementation of a fault detection technique that enables quadruped robots to identify limb damage and automatically switch to tripedal gaits, allowing continued operation despite physical impairment.

Conclusion: The proposed off-line learning method provides crucial fault detection and adaptation capability for quadruped robots in hazardous environments, bridging the gap between robotic and biological survival mechanisms by enabling automatic gait adjustment to damaged limb conditions.

Abstract: Operations in hazardous environments put humans, animals, and machines at high risk for physically damaging consequences. In contrast to humans and animals, quadruped robots cannot naturally identify and adjust their locomotion to a severely debilitated limb. The ability to detect limb damage and adjust movement to a new physical morphology is the difference between survival and death for humans and animals. The same can be said for quadruped robots autonomously carrying out remote assignments in dynamic, complex settings. This work presents the development and implementation of an off-line learning-based method to detect single limb faults from proprioceptive sensor data in a quadrupedal robot. The aim of the fault detection technique is to provide the correct output for the controller to select the appropriate tripedal gait to use given the robot's current physical morphology.

</details>


### [306] [Diffusion Policy with Bayesian Expert Selection for Active Multi-Target Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03404)
*Haotian Xiang,Qin Lu,Yaakov Bar-Shalom*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bayesian framework for uncertainty-aware expert selection in diffusion policies for active multi-target tracking, using pessimistic offline bandit approach to balance exploration vs exploitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies capture diverse behavioral strategies but lack uncertainty quantification for strategy selection, which is crucial for active multi-target tracking where robots must balance exploration for undetected targets with exploitation of uncertain tracked ones.

Method: Formulate expert selection as offline contextual bandit problem. Use multi-head Variational Bayesian Last Layer (VBLL) model to predict expected tracking performance of each expert strategy with uncertainty estimates. Apply Lower Confidence Bound (LCB) criterion for pessimistic selection, then condition diffusion policy on selected expert.

Result: Outperforms base diffusion policy and standard gating methods (Mixture-of-Experts selection and deterministic regression baselines) in simulated indoor tracking scenarios.

Conclusion: Bayesian uncertainty-aware expert selection with pessimistic offline decision-making improves active multi-target tracking performance by avoiding overcommitment to unreliable experts and better balancing exploration-exploitation trade-off.

Abstract: Active multi-target tracking requires a mobile robot to balance exploration for undetected targets with exploitation of uncertain tracked ones. Diffusion policies have emerged as a powerful approach for capturing diverse behavioral strategies by learning action sequences from expert demonstrations. However, existing methods implicitly select among strategies through the denoising process, without uncertainty quantification over which strategy to execute. We formulate expert selection for diffusion policies as an offline contextual bandit problem and propose a Bayesian framework for pessimistic, uncertainty-aware strategy selection. A multi-head Variational Bayesian Last Layer (VBLL) model predicts the expected tracking performance of each expert strategy given the current belief state, providing both a point estimate and predictive uncertainty. Following the pessimism principle for offline decision-making, a Lower Confidence Bound (LCB) criterion then selects the expert whose worst-case predicted performance is best, avoiding overcommitment to experts with unreliable predictions. The selected expert conditions a diffusion policy to generate corresponding action sequences. Experiments on simulated indoor tracking scenarios demonstrate that our approach outperforms both the base diffusion policy and standard gating methods, including Mixture-of-Experts selection and deterministic regression baselines.

</details>


### [307] [Do Robots Need Body Language? Comparing Communication Modalities for Legible Motion Intent in Human-Shared Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03451)
*Jonathan Albert Cohen,Kye Shimizu,Allen Song,Vishnu Bharath,Kent Larson,Pattie Maes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Study compares how different signaling modalities (expressive motion, lights, text, audio) affect human understanding of quadruped robot's navigation actions, measuring prediction accuracy, confidence, and trust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in shared spaces often move in ways that are difficult for humans to interpret, placing burden on people to adapt. High-DoF robots exhibit expressive motion that people read as intentional, making it important to understand how such cues are perceived.

Method: Online video study evaluating four signaling modalities (expressive motion, lights, text, audio) across four common scenarios with Boston Dynamics Spot quadruped robot. Measures human accuracy in predicting navigation actions, confidence in predictions, and trust in robot safety.

Result: The study tests how expressive motions compare to explicit channels, whether aligned multimodal cues enhance interpretability, and how conflicting cues affect user confidence and trust. Provides initial evidence on relative effectiveness of implicit vs explicit signaling strategies.

Conclusion: Research contributes to understanding how different signaling modalities shape human interpretation of robot navigation actions, with implications for designing more interpretable and trustworthy human-robot interactions in shared spaces.

Abstract: Robots in shared spaces often move in ways that are difficult for people to interpret, placing the burden on humans to adapt. High-DoF robots exhibit motion that people read as expressive, intentionally or not, making it important to understand how such cues are perceived. We present an online video study evaluating how different signaling modalities, expressive motion, lights, text, and audio, shape people's ability to understand a quadruped robot's upcoming navigation actions (Boston Dynamics Spot). Across four common scenarios, we measure how each modality influences humans' (1) accuracy in predicting the robot's next navigation action, (2) confidence in that prediction, and (3) trust in the robot to act safely. The study tests how expressive motions compare to explicit channels, whether aligned multimodal cues enhance interpretability, and how conflicting cues affect user confidence and trust. We contribute initial evidence on the relative effectiveness of implicit versus explicit signaling strategies.

</details>


### [308] [Sim2Real-AD: A Modular Sim-to-Real Framework for Deploying VLM-Guided Reinforcement Learning in Real-World Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03497)
*Zilin Huang,Zhengyang Wan,Zihao Sheng,Boyue Wang,Junwei You,Yue Leng,Sikai Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Zero-shot sim-to-real transfer framework for CARLA-trained VLM-guided RL policies to full-scale vehicles without real-world training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying simulation-trained RL policies to real autonomous vehicles is challenging due to observation/action mismatches between simulators and physical platforms.

Method: Modular framework with four components: Geometric Observation Bridge (GOB) for BEV conversion, Physics-Aware Action Mapping (PAM) for command translation, Two-Phase Progressive Training (TPT) for stable adaptation, and Real-time Deployment Pipeline (RDP) for closed-loop execution.

Result: Simulation experiments validate framework effectiveness and module contributions. Zero-shot deployment on Ford E-Transit achieves 90% success in car-following, 80% in obstacle avoidance, and 75% in stop-sign interaction.

Conclusion: First demonstration of zero-shot closed-loop deployment of CARLA-trained VLM-guided RL policy on full-scale real vehicle without real-world RL training data.

Abstract: Deploying reinforcement learning policies trained in simulation to real autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge, particularly for VLM-guided RL frameworks whose policies are typically learned with simulator-native observations and simulator-coupled action semantics that are unavailable on physical platforms. This paper presents Sim2Real-AD, a modular framework for zero-shot sim-to-real transfer of CARLA-trained VLM-guided RL policies to full-scale vehicles without any real-world RL training data. The framework decomposes the transfer problem into four components: a Geometric Observation Bridge (GOB) that converts monocular front-view images into simulator-compatible bird's-eye-view (BEV) observations, a Physics-Aware Action Mapping (PAM) that translates policy outputs into platform-agnostic physical commands, a Two-Phase Progressive Training (TPT) strategy that stabilizes adaptation by separating action-space and observation-space transfer, and a Real-time Deployment Pipeline (RDP) that integrates perception, policy inference, control conversion, and safety monitoring for closed-loop execution. Simulation experiments show that the framework preserves the relative performance ordering of representative RL algorithms across different reward paradigms and validate the contribution of each module. Zero-shot deployment on a full-scale Ford E-Transit achieves success rates of 90%, 80%, and 75% in car-following, obstacle avoidance, and stop-sign interaction scenarios, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this study is among the first to demonstrate zero-shot closed-loop deployment of a CARLA-trained VLM-guided RL policy on a full-scale real vehicle without any real-world RL training data. The demo video and code are available at: https://zilin-huang.github.io/Sim2Real-AD-website/.

</details>


### [309] [Optimizing Neurorobot Policy under Limited Demonstration Data through Preference Regret](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03523)
*Viet Dung Nguyen,Yuhang Song,Anh Nguyen,Jamison Heard,Reynold Bailey,Alexander Ororbia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MYOE framework enables robots to learn complex behaviors from limited demonstrations using self-imitation and preference-based goal estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RL from demonstrations assumes abundant expert data and IID assumptions, which are unrealistic in real-world robotics due to data scarcity, high collection costs, and compounding errors during test-time execution.

Method: Proposes the MYOE self-imitation framework with QMoP-SSM (queryable mixture-of-preferences state space model) that estimates desired goals at each time step and computes "preference regret" to optimize robot control policies.

Result: Experiments show the agent demonstrates robustness, adaptability, and superior out-of-sample performance compared to state-of-the-art RLfD methods.

Conclusion: The MYOE framework with QMoP-SSM effectively addresses data scarcity and compounding error issues in robot RL from demonstrations, enabling learning complex behaviors from limited samples.

Abstract: Robot reinforcement learning from demonstrations (RLfD) assumes that expert data is abundant; this is usually unrealistic in the real world given data scarcity as well as high collection cost. Furthermore, imitation learning algorithms assume that the data is independently and identically distributed, which ultimately results in poorer performance as gradual errors emerge and compound within test-time trajectories. We address these issues by introducing the "master your own expertise" (MYOE) framework, a self-imitation framework that enables robotic agents to learn complex behaviors from limited demonstration data samples. Inspired by human perception and action, we propose and design what we call the queryable mixture-of-preferences state space model (QMoP-SSM), which estimates the desired goal at every time step. These desired goals are used in computing the "preference regret", which is used to optimize the robot control policy. Our experiments demonstrate the robustness, adaptability, and out-of-sample performance of our agent compared to other state-of-the-art RLfD schemes. The GitHub repository that supports this work can be found at: https://github.com/rxng8/neurorobot-preference-regret-learning.

</details>


### [310] [Drift-Based Policy Optimization: Native One-Step Policy Learning for Online Robot Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03540)
*Yuxuan Gao,Yedong Shen,Shiqi Zhang,Wenhao Yu,Yifan Duan,Jia pan,Jiajia Wu,Jiajun Deng,Yanyong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DBP/DBPO: Two-stage framework for one-step generative policies that achieves diffusion-level performance with 100x faster inference, enabling high-frequency (105.2 Hz) real-world robotic control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-step generative policies (like diffusion) require many network evaluations at inference time (tens to hundreds of NFEs), making them too slow for high-frequency closed-loop control and online RL. Need one-step policies that maintain multimodal action modeling capacity.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Drift-Based Policy (DBP) - uses fixed-point drifting objectives to internalize iterative refinement into model parameters during training, creating a one-step generative backbone with multimodal capacity. 2) Drift-Based Policy Optimization (DBPO) - online RL framework that adds a compatible stochastic interface to pretrained DBP, enabling stable on-policy updates while preserving one-step deployment.

Result: DBP matches/exceeds multi-step diffusion performance with up to 100x faster inference. Outperforms existing one-step baselines on manipulation benchmarks. DBPO enables stable online policy improvement. Real-world dual-arm robot achieves 105.2 Hz high-frequency control.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully shifts refinement from inference to training, enabling native one-step generative policies that combine the benefits of multimodal action modeling with fast inference suitable for high-frequency control and online RL.

Abstract: Although multi-step generative policies achieve strong performance in robotic manipulation by modeling multimodal action distributions, they require multi-step iterative denoising at inference time. Each action therefore needs tens to hundreds of network function evaluations (NFEs), making them costly for high-frequency closed-loop control and online reinforcement learning (RL). To address this limitation, we propose a two-stage framework for native one-step generative policies that shifts refinement from inference to training. First, we introduce the Drift-Based Policy (DBP), which leverages fixed-point drifting objectives to internalize iterative refinement into the model parameters, yielding a one-step generative backbone by design while preserving multimodal action modeling capacity. Second, we develop Drift-Based Policy Optimization (DBPO), an online RL framework that equips the pretrained backbone with a compatible stochastic interface, enabling stable on-policy updates without sacrificing the one-step deployment property. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework across offline imitation learning, online fine-tuning, and real-world control scenarios. DBP matches or exceeds the performance of multi-step diffusion policies while achieving up to $100\times$ faster inference. It also consistently outperforms existing one-step baselines on challenging manipulation benchmarks. Moreover, DBPO enables effective and stable policy improvement in online settings. Experiments on a real-world dual-arm robot demonstrate reliable high-frequency control at 105.2 Hz.

</details>


### [311] [CRAFT: Video Diffusion for Bimanual Robot Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03552)
*Jason Chen,I-Chun Arthur Liu,Gaurav Sukhatme,Daniel Seita*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CRAFT uses video diffusion models to generate diverse bimanual robot demonstrations from simulated trajectories, improving policy robustness without real-world replay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world bimanual robot demonstrations are costly and lack visual diversity, limiting policy generalization across viewpoints, objects, and robot embodiments.

Method: CRAFT conditions video diffusion on edge cues from simulator trajectories to generate photorealistic manipulation videos with action labels, enabling unified augmentation across object poses, viewpoints, lighting, backgrounds, and embodiments.

Result: CRAFT improves success rates over existing augmentation methods and simple data scaling in both simulated and real-world bimanual tasks, demonstrating enhanced generalization.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based video generation can substantially expand demonstration diversity and improve generalization for dual-arm manipulation tasks, bypassing the need for costly real-world data collection.

Abstract: Bimanual robot learning from demonstrations is fundamentally limited by the cost and narrow visual diversity of real-world data, which constrains policy robustness across viewpoints, object configurations, and embodiments. We present Canny-guided Robot Data Generation using Video Diffusion Transformers (CRAFT), a video diffusion-based framework for scalable bimanual demonstration generation that synthesizes temporally coherent manipulation videos while producing action labels. By conditioning video diffusion on edge-based structural cues extracted from simulator-generated trajectories, CRAFT produces physically plausible trajectory variations and supports a unified augmentation pipeline spanning object pose changes, camera viewpoints, lighting and background variations, cross-embodiment transfer, and multi-view synthesis. We leverage a pre-trained video diffusion model to convert simulated videos, along with action labels from the simulation trajectories, into action-consistent demonstrations. Starting from only a few real-world demonstrations, CRAFT generates a large, visually diverse set of photorealistic training data, bypassing the need to replay demonstrations on the real robot (Sim2Real). Across simulated and real-world bimanual tasks, CRAFT improves success rates over existing augmentation strategies and straightforward data scaling, demonstrating that diffusion-based video generation can substantially expand demonstration diversity and improve generalization for dual-arm manipulation tasks. Our project website is available at: https://craftaug.github.io/

</details>


### [312] [HAD: Combining Hierarchical Diffusion with Metric-Decoupled RL for End-to-End Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03581)
*Wenhao Yao,Xinglong Sun,Zhenxin Li,Shiyi Lan,Zi Wang,Jose M. Alvarez,Zuxuan Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HAD is an end-to-end autonomous driving planning framework with hierarchical diffusion policy that decomposes planning into coarse-to-fine process, using structure-preserved trajectory expansion and metric-decoupled policy optimization to achieve state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end planning models face challenges: scoring-selection frameworks struggle with optimizing selection from large candidate spaces, diffusion-based decoding introduces unrealistic trajectories, and RL approaches use single coupled rewards without structured signals, limiting optimization effectiveness.

Method: 1) Hierarchical Diffusion Policy for coarse-to-fine planning decomposition; 2) Structure-Preserved Trajectory Expansion to generate realistic candidates while maintaining kinematic structure; 3) Metric-Decoupled Policy Optimization (MDPO) for structured RL optimization across multiple driving objectives.

Result: HAD achieves new state-of-the-art performance on both NAVSIM and HUGSIM benchmarks: +2.3 EPDMS improvement on NAVSIM and +4.9 Route Completion improvement on HUGSIM, outperforming prior methods by significant margins.

Conclusion: The proposed HAD framework effectively addresses limitations in current end-to-end planning approaches through hierarchical decomposition, structure-preserved trajectory generation, and decoupled multi-objective optimization, demonstrating superior performance in autonomous driving planning tasks.

Abstract: End-to-end planning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for autonomous driving, where recent models often adopt a scoring-selection framework to choose trajectories from a large set of candidates, with diffusion-based decoding showing strong promise. However, directly selecting from the entire candidate space remains difficult to optimize, and Gaussian perturbations used in diffusion often introduce unrealistic trajectories that complicate the denoising process. In addition, for training these models, reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise, but existing end-to-end RL approaches typically rely on a single coupled reward without structured signals, limiting optimization effectiveness. To address these challenges, we propose HAD, an end-to-end planning framework with a Hierarchical Diffusion Policy that decomposes planning into a coarse-to-fine process. To improve trajectory generation, we introduce Structure-Preserved Trajectory Expansion, which produces realistic candidates while maintaining kinematic structure. For policy learning, we develop Metric-Decoupled Policy Optimization (MDPO) to enable structured RL optimization across multiple driving objectives. Extensive experiments show that HAD achieves new state-of-the-art performance on both NAVSIM and HUGSIM, outperforming prior arts by a huge margin: +2.3 EPDMS on NAVSIM and +4.9 Route Completion on HUGSIM.

</details>


### [313] [Human-Robot Copilot for Data-Efficient Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03613)
*Rui Yan,Zaitian Gongye,Lars Paulsen,Xuxin Cheng,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Human-Robot Copilot framework enables dexterous teleoperation with scaling factor while maintaining compatibility across various manipulators, improving policy performance with fewer demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interactive imitation learning methods struggle to balance dexterity and generality - they either provide fine-grained corrections limited to specific kinematic structures, or achieve generality at the cost of precise control, especially when limited demonstrations are available and policies face out-of-distribution states.

Method: Proposes Human-Robot Copilot framework that leverages a scaling factor for dexterous teleoperation while maintaining compatibility with a wide range of industrial and research manipulators, building on but improving the Human-Gated DAgger paradigm.

Result: Experimental results show the framework achieves higher performance with the same number of demonstration trajectories, and since corrective interventions are required only intermittently, the overall data collection process is more efficient and less time-consuming.

Conclusion: The Human-Robot Copilot framework successfully overcomes the dexterity-generality trade-off in interactive imitation learning, enabling both precise control and broad compatibility while improving data collection efficiency.

Abstract: Collecting human demonstrations via teleoperation is a common approach for teaching robots task-specific skills. However, when only a limited number of demonstrations are available, policies are prone to entering out-of-distribution (OOD) states due to compounding errors or environmental stochasticity. Existing interactive imitation learning or human-in-the-loop methods try to address this issue by following the Human-Gated DAgger (HG-DAgger) paradigm, an approach that augments demonstrations through selective human intervention during policy execution. Nevertheless, these approaches struggle to balance dexterity and generality: they either provide fine-grained corrections but are limited to specific kinematic structures, or achieve generality at the cost of precise control. To overcome this limitation, we propose the Human-Robot Copilot framework that can leverage a scaling factor for dexterous teleoperation while maintaining compatibility with a wide range of industrial and research manipulators. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework achieves higher performance with the same number of demonstration trajectories. Moreover, since corrective interventions are required only intermittently, the overall data collection process is more efficient and less time-consuming.

</details>


### [314] [Towards Edge Intelligence via Autonomous Navigation: A Robot-Assisted Data Collection Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03623)
*Tingting Huang,Yingyang Chen,Sixian Qin,Zhijian Lin,Jun Li,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A communication-and-learning dual-driven autonomous navigation scheme for mobile robots in NLoS environments that optimizes navigation, communication, and learning performance simultaneously.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing demand for large-scale, high-quality data in edge intelligence systems requires mobile robots to collect data proactively in complex environments, but existing methods struggle with reliable and efficient performance in non-line-of-sight conditions.

Method: Proposes a CLD scheme incorporating region-aware propagation characteristics and non-point-mass robot representation, with an efficient MM-based algorithm to solve the non-convex, non-smooth optimization problem.

Result: Superior performance in collision-avoidance navigation, data collection, and model training compared to benchmarks, with adaptability to different scenarios through flexible weight adjustment.

Conclusion: The CLD scheme effectively addresses challenges in robot-assisted data collection for edge intelligence, enabling simultaneous optimization of multiple objectives in complex NLoS environments.

Abstract: With the growing demand for large-scale and high-quality data in edge intelligence systems, mobile robots are increasingly deployed to collect data proactively, particularly in complex environments. However, existing robot-assisted data collection methods face significant challenges in achieving reliable and efficient performance, especially in non-line-of-sight (NLoS) environments. This paper proposes a communication-and-learning dual-driven (CLD) autonomous navigation scheme that incorporates region-aware propagation characteristics and a non-point-mass robot representation. This scheme enables simultaneous optimization of navigation, communication, and learning performance. An efficient algorithm based on majorization-minimization (MM) is proposed to solve the non-convex and non-smooth CLD problem. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves superior performance in collision-avoidance navigation, data collection, and model training compared to benchmark methods. It is also shown that CLD can adapt to different scenarios by flexibly adjusting the weight factor among navigation, communication and learning objectives.

</details>


### [315] [A Multi-View 3D Telepresence System for XR Robot Teleoperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03730)
*Enes Ulas Dincer,Manuel Zaremski,Alexandra Nick,Elias Wucher,Barbara Deml,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-view VR telepresence system combines 3D point clouds from multiple cameras with wrist-mounted RGB stream for improved robot teleoperation, outperforming other visualization methods in manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot teleoperation requires intuitive 3D visualization with reliable depth cues, which conventional screen-based interfaces fail to provide. Current methods lack the combination of global 3D structure with localized high-resolution detail needed for effective manipulation tasks.

Method: Developed a multi-view VR telepresence system that: (1) fuses geometry from three cameras to produce GPU-accelerated point-cloud rendering on standalone VR hardware (Meta Quest 3), and (2) integrates a wrist-mounted RGB stream to provide high-resolution local detail where point-cloud accuracy is limited. The pipeline supports real-time rendering of approximately 75k points.

Result: In a within-subject study with 31 participants across three teleoperated manipulation tasks, the system achieved best overall performance in task success, completion time, perceived workload, and usability. Point Cloud modality without RGB also outperformed RGB streams and OpenTeleVision.

Conclusion: Combining global 3D structure with localized high-resolution detail substantially improves telepresence for manipulation and provides a strong foundation for next-generation robot teleoperation systems.

Abstract: Robot teleoperation is critical for applications such as remote maintenance, fleet robotics, search and rescue, and data collection for robot learning. Effective teleoperation requires intuitive 3D visualization with reliable depth cues, which conventional screen-based interfaces often fail to provide. We introduce a multi-view VR telepresence system that (1) fuses geometry from three cameras to produce GPU-accelerated point-cloud rendering on standalone VR hardware, and (2) integrates a wrist-mounted RGB stream to provide high-resolution local detail where point-cloud accuracy is limited. Our pipeline supports real-time rendering of approximately 75k points on the Meta Quest 3. A within-subject study was conducted with 31 participants to compare our system to other visualisation modalities, such as RGB streams, a projection of stereo-vision directly in the VR device and point clouds without providing additional RGB information. Across three different teleoperated manipulation tasks, we measured task success, completion time, perceived workload, and usability. Our system achieved the best overall performance, while the Point Cloud modality without RGB also outperforming the RGB streams and OpenTeleVision. These results show that combining global 3D structure with localized high-resolution detail substantially improves telepresence for manipulation and provides a strong foundation for next-generation robot teleoperation systems.

</details>


### [316] [CT-VoxelMap: Efficient Continuous-Time LiDAR-Inertial Odometry with Probabilistic Adaptive Voxel Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03747)
*Lei Zhao,Xingyi Li,Tianchen Deng,Chuan Cao,Han Zhang,Weidong Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A continuous-time LiDAR-inertial odometry method using B-splines on matrix Lie groups with improved Jacobian derivation, online fitting error estimation, and hybrid map management for better accuracy and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maintaining stable and accurate localization during fast motion or on rough terrain is challenging for resource-constrained mobile robots. Existing B-spline methods either treat control points as variables (complex Jacobians) or estimate in quaternion space, often ignoring fitting errors between spline and true trajectory.

Method: 1) Represent control point increments on matrix Lie groups as variables, leveraging cumulative B-spline form for simpler analytical Jacobians. 2) Use IMU forward propagation to estimate fitting errors online. 3) Introduce hybrid feature-based voxel map management. 4) Implement re-estimation policy for computational efficiency and robustness.

Result: Evaluated on multiple challenging public datasets, demonstrating superior performance on most sequences. Ablation studies show impact of each module on pose estimation system.

Conclusion: The proposed continuous-time LiDAR-inertial odometry method effectively addresses challenges of fast motion and rough terrain with improved accuracy, robustness, and computational efficiency through novel B-spline representation, online error estimation, and intelligent map management.

Abstract: Maintaining stable and accurate localization during fast motion or on rough terrain remains highly challenging for mobile robots with onboard resources. Currently, multi-sensor fusion methods based on continuous-time representation offer a potential and effective solution to this challenge. Among these, spline-based methods provide an efficient and intuitive approach for continuous-time representation. Previous continuous-time odometry works based on B-splines either treat control points as variables to be estimated or perform estimation in quaternion space, which introduces complexity in deriving analytical Jacobians and often overlooks the fitting error between the spline and the true trajectory over time. To address these issues, we first propose representing the increments of control points on matrix Lie groups as variables to be estimated. Leveraging the feature of the cumulative form of B-splines, we derive a more compact formulation that yields simpler analytical Jacobians without requiring additional boundary condition considerations. Second, we utilize forward propagation information from IMU measurements to estimate fitting errors online and further introduce a hybrid feature-based voxel map management strategy, enhancing system accuracy and robustness. Finally, we propose a re-estimation policy that significantly improves system computational efficiency and robustness. The proposed method is evaluated on multiple challenging public datasets, demonstrating superior performance on most sequences. Detailed ablation studies are conducted to analyze the impact of each module on the overall pose estimation system.

</details>


### [317] [Build on Priors: Vision--Language--Guided Neuro-Symbolic Imitation Learning for Data-Efficient Real-World Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03759)
*Pierrick Lorang,Johannes Huemer,Timothy Duggan,Kai Goebel,Patrik Zips,Matthias Scheutz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic framework that learns manipulation tasks from few demonstrations by autonomously constructing symbolic planning domains and control policies without manual engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enabling robots to learn long-horizon manipulation tasks from minimal demonstrations remains challenging. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches require hand-crafted abstractions, labeled data, or large datasets, limiting scalability and real-world applicability.

Method: Segments demonstrations into skills, uses Vision-Language Model (VLM) to classify skills and identify equivalent states, constructs state-transition graph, processes with Answer Set Programming to synthesize PDDL planning domain, learns control policies at reference level, enables data augmentation via known controllers.

Result: Validated on real industrial forklift with statistically rigorous trials and cross-platform on Kinova Gen3 robotic arm across two benchmarks. Shows practical path toward scalable, data-efficient, expert-free neuro-symbolic robotics.

Conclusion: Grounding control learning, VLM-driven abstraction, and automated planning synthesis into a unified pipeline enables scalable, data-efficient, expert-free, and interpretable neuro-symbolic robotics.

Abstract: Enabling robots to learn long-horizon manipulation tasks from a handful of demonstrations remains a central challenge in robotics. Existing neuro-symbolic approaches often rely on hand-crafted symbolic abstractions, semantically labeled trajectories or large demonstration datasets, limiting their scalability and real-world applicability. We present a scalable neuro-symbolic framework that autonomously constructs symbolic planning domains and data-efficient control policies from as few as one to thirty unannotated skill demonstrations, without requiring manual domain engineering. Our method segments demonstrations into skills and employs a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to classify skills and identify equivalent high-level states, enabling automatic construction of a state-transition graph. This graph is processed by an Answer Set Programming solver to synthesize a PDDL planning domain, which an oracle function exploits to isolate the minimal, task-relevant and target relative observation and action spaces for each skill policy. Policies are learned at the control reference level rather than at the raw actuator signal level, yielding a smoother and less noisy learning target. Known controllers can be leveraged for real-world data augmentation by projecting a single demonstration onto other objects in the scene, simultaneously enriching the graph construction process and the dataset for imitation learning. We validate our framework primarily on a real industrial forklift across statistically rigorous manipulation trials, and demonstrate cross-platform generality on a Kinova Gen3 robotic arm across two standard benchmarks. Our results show that grounding control learning, VLM-driven abstraction, and automated planning synthesis into a unified pipeline constitutes a practical path toward scalable, data-efficient, expert-free and interpretable neuro-symbolic robotics.

</details>


### [318] [A Novel Hybrid PID-LQR Controller for Sit-To-Stand Assistance Using a CAD-Integrated Simscape Multibody Lower Limb Exoskeleton](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03766)
*Ranjeet Kumbhar,Rajmeet Singh,Appaso M Gadade,Ashish Singla,Irfan Hussain*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel Hybrid PID-LQR controller for lower limb exoskeletons achieves superior sit-to-stand transition performance with 70%+ RMSE reduction over PID controllers and 90% faster settling time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precise control of lower limb exoskeletons during sit-to-stand transitions is challenging due to nonlinear human-exoskeleton dynamics and strict clinical safety requirements for trajectory tracking.

Method: Three control strategies were compared: PID, LQR, and a novel Hybrid PID-LQR controller. A high-fidelity physics-based model was built from SolidWorks CAD in MATLAB/Simulink. Reference joint trajectories were generated using OpenSim and decomposed into three biomechanical phases. The hybrid controller combines LQR's optimal transient response with PID's integral disturbance rejection using a blending coefficient α=0.65.

Result: Hybrid PID-LQR achieved RMSE reductions of 72.3% (hip) and 70.4% (knee) over PID, reduced settling time by over 90% across all joints, and limited overshoot to 2.39%-6.10%, outperforming both baseline controllers across all performance metrics.

Conclusion: The Hybrid PID-LQR controller demonstrates superior performance for sit-to-stand transitions in lower limb exoskeletons, showing strong translational potential for clinical assistive exoskeleton deployment due to its precise trajectory tracking and robust disturbance rejection.

Abstract: Precise control of lower limb exoskeletons during sit-to-stand (STS) transitions remains a central challenge in rehabilitation robotics owing to the highly nonlinear, time-varying dynamics of the human-exoskeleton system and the stringent trajectory tracking requirements imposed by clinical safety. This paper presents the systematic design, simulation, and comparative evaluation of three control strategies: a classical Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller, a Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR), and a novel Hybrid PID-LQR controller applied to a bilateral lower limb exoskeleton performing the sit-to-stand transition. A high-fidelity, physics-based dynamic model of the exoskeleton is constructed by importing a SolidWorks CAD assembly directly into the MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Multibody environment, preserving accurate geometric and inertial properties of all links. Physiologically representative reference joint trajectories for the hip, knee, and ankle joints are generated using OpenSim musculoskeletal simulation and decomposed into three biomechanical phases: flexion-momentum (0-33%), momentum-transfer (34-66%), and extension (67-100%). The proposed Hybrid PID-LQR controller combines the optimal transient response of LQR with the integral disturbance rejection of PID through a tuned blending coefficient alpha = 0.65. Simulation results demonstrate that the Hybrid PID-LQR achieves RMSE reductions of 72.3% and 70.4% over PID at the hip and knee joints, respectively, reduces settling time by over 90% relative to PID across all joints, and limits overshoot to 2.39%-6.10%, confirming its superiority over both baseline strategies across all evaluated performance metrics and demonstrating strong translational potential for clinical assistive exoskeleton deployment.

</details>


### [319] [OpenRC: An Open-Source Robotic Colonoscopy Framework for Multimodal Data Acquisition and Autonomy Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03781)
*Siddhartha Kapuria,Mohammad Rafiee Javazm,Naruhiko Ikoma,Joga Ivatury,Mohammad Ali Nasseri,Nassir Navab,Farshid Alambeigi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OpenRC is an open-source robotic colonoscopy framework that enables reproducible research by recording multimodal data (video, commands, actuation, tip pose) during clinical procedures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current colonoscopy platforms lack systematic support for studying operator control, instrument motion, and visual feedback dynamics, limiting reproducible research in robotic colonoscopy, medical imaging, and vision-language-action learning.

Method: Developed OpenRC, an open-source modular framework that retrofits conventional scopes while preserving clinical workflow. It records video, operator commands, actuation state, and distal tip pose simultaneously, with experimental validation of motion consistency and cross-modal latency.

Result: Collected a multimodal dataset of 1,894 teleoperated episodes (~19 hours) across 10 structured task variations including routine navigation, failure events, and recovery behaviors. Validated motion consistency and quantified latency across sensing streams.

Conclusion: OpenRC provides a reproducible foundation for multimodal robotic colonoscopy research by unifying open hardware with aligned multimodal datasets, enabling advances in surgical autonomy and related fields.

Abstract: Colorectal cancer screening critically depends on colonoscopy, yet existing platforms offer limited support for systematically studying the coupled dynamics of operator control, instrument motion, and visual feedback. This gap restricts reproducible closed-loop research in robotic colonoscopy, medical imaging, and emerging vision-language-action (VLA) learning paradigms. To address this challenge, we present OpenRC, an open-source modular robotic colonoscopy framework that retrofits conventional scopes while preserving clinical workflow. The framework supports simultaneous recording of video, operator commands, actuation state, and distal tip pose. We experimentally validated motion consistency and quantified cross-modal latency across sensing streams. Using this platform, we collected a multimodal dataset comprising 1,894 teleoperated episodes ~19 hours across 10 structured task variations of routine navigation, failure events, and recovery behaviors. By unifying open hardware and an aligned multimodal dataset, OpenRC provides a reproducible foundation for research in multimodal robotic colonoscopy and surgical autonomy.

</details>


### [320] [From Prompt to Physical Action: Structured Backdoor Attacks on LLM-Mediated Robotic Control Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03890)
*Mingyang Xie,Jin Wei-Kocsis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LLM-mediated robotic control systems are vulnerable to structured backdoor attacks during fine-tuning, with attacks targeting JSON command formats being most effective. A defense using secondary LLM verification reduces attack success but introduces significant latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The integration of LLMs into robotic control pipelines enables natural language interfaces but introduces underexplored vulnerabilities to structured backdoor attacks during fine-tuning, creating a critical digital-to-physical security gap.

Method: Researchers experimentally investigate LoRA-based supply-chain backdoors in LLM-mediated ROS2 robotic control systems. They construct two poisoned fine-tuning strategies targeting different stages of the command generation pipeline and evaluate attacks in both simulation and real-world experiments. They also implement an agentic verification defense using a secondary LLM for semantic consistency checking.

Result: Backdoors embedded at natural-language reasoning stage don't reliably propagate to executable outputs, while backdoors aligned directly with structured JSON command formats successfully trigger physical actions. Backdoored models achieve 83% Attack Success Rate while maintaining 93% Clean Performance Accuracy and sub-second latency. The secondary LLM defense reduces ASR to 20% but increases latency to 8-9 seconds.

Conclusion: The research highlights structural vulnerabilities in LLM-mediated robotic control architectures and underscores the need for robotics-aware defenses for embodied AI systems, revealing a significant security-responsiveness trade-off in real-time robotic systems.

Abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into robotic control pipelines enables natural language interfaces that translate user prompts into executable commands. However, this digital-to-physical interface introduces a critical and underexplored vulnerability: structured backdoor attacks embedded during fine-tuning. In this work, we experimentally investigate LoRA-based supply-chain backdoors in LLM-mediated ROS2 robotic control systems and evaluate their impact on physical robot execution. We construct two poisoned fine-tuning strategies targeting different stages of the command generation pipeline and reveal a key systems-level insight: back-doors embedded at the natural-language reasoning stage do not reliably propagate to executable control outputs, whereas backdoors aligned directly with structured JSON command formats successfully survive translation and trigger physical actions. In both simulation and real-world experiments, backdoored models achieve an average Attack Success Rate of 83% while maintaining over 93% Clean Performance Accuracy (CPA) and sub-second latency, demonstrating both reliability and stealth. We further implement an agentic verification defense using a secondary LLM for semantic consistency checking. Although this reduces the Attack Success Rate (ASR) to 20%, it increases end-to-end latency to 8-9 seconds, exposing a significant security-responsiveness trade-off in real-time robotic systems. These results highlight structural vulnerabilities in LLM-mediated robotic control architectures and underscore the need for robotics-aware defenses for embodied AI systems.

</details>


### [321] [DC-Ada: Reward-Only Decentralized Observation-Interface Adaptation for Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Teams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03905)
*Saad Alqithami*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DC-Ada is a decentralized adaptation method that adapts per-robot observation transforms to handle heterogeneous sensing in multi-robot teams without modifying the shared policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous robot teams with different sensing capabilities cause pretrained controllers to degrade when deployed, even when tasks and action interfaces remain unchanged.

Method: Keeps pretrained shared policy frozen and adapts compact per-robot observation transforms using gradient-free, communication-minimal random search with budgeted accept/reject and common-random-number rollouts.

Result: DC-Ada improves task completion in severe coverage-based mapping scenarios while requiring only scalar team returns and no policy fine-tuning or persistent communication.

Conclusion: DC-Ada offers a practical deploy-time adaptation method for heterogeneous multi-robot teams, providing a complementary approach to existing mitigation strategies.

Abstract: Heterogeneity is a defining feature of deployed multi-robot teams: platforms often differ in sensing modalities, ranges, fields of view, and failure patterns. Controllers trained under nominal sensing can degrade sharply when deployed on robots with missing or mismatched sensors, even when the task and action interface are unchanged. We present DC-Ada, a reward-only decentralized adaptation method that keeps a pretrained shared policy frozen and instead adapts compact per-robot observation transforms to map heterogeneous sensing into a fixed inference interface. DC-Ada is gradient-free and communication-minimal: it uses budgeted accept/reject random search with short common-random-number rollouts under a strict step budget. We evaluate DC-Ada against four baselines in a deterministic 2D multi-robot simulator covering warehouse logistics, search and rescue, and collaborative mapping, across four heterogeneity regimes (H0--H3) and five seeds with a matched budget of $200{,}000$ joint environment steps per run. Results show that heterogeneity can substantially degrade a frozen shared policy and that no single mitigation dominates across all tasks and metrics. Observation normalization is strongest for reward robustness in warehouse logistics and competitive in search and rescue, while the frozen shared policy is strongest for reward in collaborative mapping. DC-Ada offers a useful complementary operating point: it improves completion most clearly in severe coverage-based mapping while requiring only scalar team returns and no policy fine-tuning or persistent communication. These results position DC-Ada as a practical deploy-time adaptation method for heterogeneous teams.

</details>


### [322] [VA-FastNavi-MARL: Real-Time Robot Control with Multimedia-Driven Meta-Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03998)
*Yang Zhang,Shengxi Jing,Fengxiang Wang,Yuan Feng,Hong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VA-FastNavi-MARL is a framework for real-time human-robot interaction that aligns asynchronous audio-visual inputs into unified representations using Meta-Reinforcement Learning for rapid adaptation to diverse instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for interpreting dynamic, heterogeneous multimedia commands with real-time responsiveness in human-robot interaction, addressing limitations of approaches bottlenecked by heavy sensory processing and high latency.

Method: Aligns asynchronous audio-visual inputs into unified latent representation, treats diverse instructions as distribution of navigable goals via Meta-Reinforcement Learning, uses modality-agnostic stream for low-latency control.

Result: Significantly outperforms baselines in sample efficiency, maintains robust real-time execution under noisy multimedia streams, enables rapid adaptation to unseen directives with negligible inference overhead.

Conclusion: VA-FastNavi-MARL provides an effective solution for real-time human-robot interaction with multimedia commands, offering superior performance, efficiency, and robustness compared to existing approaches.

Abstract: Interpreting dynamic, heterogeneous multimedia commands with real-time responsiveness is critical for Human-Robot Interaction. We present VA-FastNavi-MARL, a framework that aligns asynchronous audio-visual inputs into a unified latent representation. By treating diverse instructions as a distribution of navigable goals via Meta-Reinforcement Learning, our method enables rapid adaptation to unseen directives with negligible inference overhead. Unlike approaches bottlenecked by heavy sensory processing, our modality-agnostic stream ensures seamless, low-latency control. Validation on a multi-arm workspace confirms that VA-FastNavi-MARL significantly outperforms baselines in sample efficiency and maintains robust, real-time execution even under noisy multimedia streams.

</details>


### [323] [Dynamic Whole-Body Dancing with Humanoid Robots -- A Model-Based Control Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03999)
*Shibowen Zhang,Jiayang Wu,Guannan Liu,Helin Zhu,Junjie Liu,Zhehan Li,Junhong Guo,Xiaokun Leng,Hangxin Liu,Jingwen Zhang,Jikai Wang,Zonghai Chen,Zhicheng He,Jiayi Wang,Yao Su*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Integrated model-based framework for generating and executing dynamic whole-body dance motions on humanoid robots using two-stage approach with offline motion generation and online MPC-based execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable humanoid robots to perform dynamic, expressive dance motions in real-world environments by combining offline motion planning with online adaptive execution, addressing challenges of motion retargeting, dynamic feasibility, and real-time disturbance adaptation.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Offline motion generation using MoCap data retargeting via QP optimization and trajectory optimization for dynamic feasibility; 2) Online execution using centroidal dynamics-based MPC with proactive swing foot adjustment for disturbance adaptation.

Result: Successfully demonstrated on Kuavo 4Pro humanoid robot in both simulation and a four-minute live public performance with four robots, showing that longer prediction horizons improve motion expressiveness in planning and stability in execution.

Conclusion: The integrated model-based framework enables robust, dynamic dance motions on humanoid robots, with the two-stage approach effectively combining offline motion generation with online adaptive execution for real-world performance.

Abstract: This paper presents an integrated model-based framework for generating and executing dynamic whole-body dance motions on humanoid robots.
