The framework operates in two stages: offline motion generation and online motion execution, both leveraging future state prediction to enable robust and dynamic dance motions in real-world environments. In the offline motion generation stage, human dance demonstrations are captured via a motion capture (MoCap) system, retargeted to the robot by solving a Quadratic Programming (QP) problem, and further refined using Trajectory Optimization (TO) to ensure dynamic feasibility. In the online motion execution stage, a centroidal dynamics-based Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework tracks the planned motions in real time and proactively adjusts swing foot placement to adapt to real world disturbances. We validate our framework on the full-size humanoid robot Kuavo 4Pro, demonstrating the dynamic dance motions both in simulation and in a four-minute live public performance with a team of four robots. Experimental results show that longer prediction horizons improve both motion expressiveness in planning and stability in execution.

</details>


### [324] [Adapting Neural Robot Dynamics on the Fly for Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04039)
*Abdullah Altawaitan,Nikolay Atanasov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Fast adaptation of neural robot dynamics models combining offline training with efficient online updates for robust predictive control in novel conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physics-based models are too simple for real-world effects, while data-driven models require intensive data and slow training. Need for models that can quickly adapt to novel operational conditions.

Method: Learn incremental neural dynamics model offline, then perform low-rank second-order parameter adaptation online for rapid updates without full retraining.

Result: Demonstrated on real quadrotor robot, achieving robust predictive tracking control in novel operational conditions.

Conclusion: Combining offline training with efficient online updates enables fast adaptation of neural robot dynamics models for improved predictive control in changing environments.

Abstract: Accurate dynamics models are critical for the design of predictive controller for autonomous mobile robots. Physics-based models are often too simple to capture relevant real-world effects, while data-driven models are data-intensive and slow to train. We introduce an approach for fast adaptation of neural robot dynamic models that combines offline training with efficient online updates. Our approach learns an incremental neural dynamics model offline and performs low-rank second-order parameter adaptation online, enabling rapid updates without full retraining. We demonstrate the approach on a real quadrotor robot, achieving robust predictive tracking control in novel operational conditions.

</details>


### [325] [Efficient Onboard Spacecraft Pose Estimation with Event Cameras and Neuromorphic Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04117)
*Arunkumar Rathinam,Jules Lecomte,Jost Reelsen,Gregor Lenz,Axel von Arnim,Djamila Aouada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: First demonstration of spacecraft 6-DoF pose estimation using event cameras and Akida neuromorphic hardware, achieving real-time, low-power inference for autonomous space operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Space imagery is challenging due to extreme illumination, high contrast, and fast motion, which cause traditional frame-based cameras to saturate or blur. Event cameras and neuromorphic processors offer advantages for reliable relative pose estimation in autonomous rendezvous operations.

Method: Developed a pipeline coupling event-based vision with BrainChip Akida neuromorphic processor. Trained MobileNet-style keypoint regression networks on SPADES dataset using lightweight event-frame representations, applied quantization-aware training (8/4-bit), and converted models to Akida-compatible spiking neural networks. Tested three event representations and designed heatmap-based model for Akida V2.

Result: Demonstrated real-time, low-power inference on Akida V1 hardware and improved pose accuracy with heatmap-based model on Akida V2 (evaluated on Akida Cloud). Achieved practical low-latency, low-power perception for space missions.

Conclusion: This is the first end-to-end demonstration of spacecraft pose estimation running on Akida hardware, providing a practical route to low-latency, low-power perception for future autonomous space missions.

Abstract: Reliable relative pose estimation is a key enabler for autonomous rendezvous and proximity operations, yet space imagery is notoriously challenging due to extreme illumination, high contrast, and fast target motion. Event cameras provide asynchronous, change-driven measurements that can remain informative when frame-based imagery saturates or blurs, while neuromorphic processors can exploit sparse activations for low-latency, energy-efficient inferences. This paper presents a spacecraft 6-DoF pose-estimation pipeline that couples event-based vision with the BrainChip Akida neuromorphic processor. Using the SPADES dataset, we train compact MobileNet-style keypoint regression networks on lightweight event-frame representations, apply quantization-aware training (8/4-bit), and convert the models to Akida-compatible spiking neural networks. We benchmark three event representations and demonstrate real-time, low-power inference on Akida V1 hardware. We additionally design a heatmap-based model targeting Akida V2 and evaluate it on Akida Cloud, yielding improved pose accuracy. To our knowledge, this is the first end-to-end demonstration of spacecraft pose estimation running on Akida hardware, highlighting a practical route to low-latency, low-power perception for future autonomous space missions.

</details>


### [326] [Learning Dexterous Grasping from Sparse Taxonomy Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04138)
*Juhan Park,Taerim Yoon,Seungmin Kim,Joonggil Kim,Wontae Ye,Jeongeun Park,Yoonbyung Chai,Geonwoo Cho,Geunwoo Cho,Dohyeong Kim,Kyungjae Lee,Yongjae Kim,Sungjoon Choi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GRIT is a two-stage framework for dexterous manipulation that uses sparse taxonomy guidance instead of dense pose/contact specifications, improving generalization and controllability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional grasp planning requires impractical dense pose/contact specifications for every object-task combination, while end-to-end RL lacks controllability for user intervention during failures.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Predicts taxonomy-based grasp specification from scene/task context, 2) Policy generates continuous finger motions conditioned on this sparse command while preserving intended grasp structure.

Result: Shows certain grasp taxonomies are more effective for specific object geometries. GRIT improves generalization to novel objects over baselines with 87.9% overall success rate. Real-world experiments demonstrate controllability through high-level taxonomy selection.

Conclusion: GRIT enables practical dexterous manipulation by combining sparse taxonomy guidance with learned control, offering both generalization to novel objects and user controllability through high-level grasp strategy adjustment.

Abstract: Dexterous manipulation requires planning a grasp configuration suited to the object and task, which is then executed through coordinated multi-finger control. However, specifying grasp plans with dense pose or contact targets for every object and task is impractical. Meanwhile, end-to-end reinforcement learning from task rewards alone lacks controllability, making it difficult for users to intervene when failures occur. To this end, we present GRIT, a two-stage framework that learns dexterous control from sparse taxonomy guidance. GRIT first predicts a taxonomy-based grasp specification from the scene and task context. Conditioned on this sparse command, a policy generates continuous finger motions that accomplish the task while preserving the intended grasp structure. Our result shows that certain grasp taxonomies are more effective for specific object geometries. By leveraging this relationship, GRIT improves generalization to novel objects over baselines and achieves an overall success rate of 87.9%. Moreover, real-world experiments demonstrate controllability, enabling grasp strategies to be adjusted through high-level taxonomy selection based on object geometry and task intent.

</details>


### [327] [Adaptive Action Chunking at Inference-time for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04161)
*Yuanchang Liang,Xiaobo Wang,Kai Wang,Shuo Wang,Xiaojiang Peng,Haoyu Chen,David Kim Huat Chua,Prahlad Vadakkepat*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) for VLA models, using action entropy to dynamically adjust chunk sizes instead of using fixed lengths, improving robotic manipulation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models use fixed action chunk sizes at inference time, which creates a trade-off: large chunks reduce responsiveness to new information, while small chunks cause mode-jumping and jerky behavior. This fixed approach limits performance and scalability across diverse manipulation tasks.

Method: Proposes Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) strategy that uses action entropy as a cue to dynamically determine optimal chunk size based on current predictions, allowing the model to adapt chunking to task requirements.

Result: Extensive experiments on simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks show substantial performance improvements over state-of-the-art alternatives with fixed chunking approaches.

Conclusion: Adaptive action chunking using action entropy effectively balances reactivity and consistency in VLA models, overcoming limitations of fixed chunk sizes and enhancing robotic manipulation capabilities across diverse tasks.

Abstract: In Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, action chunking (i.e., executing a sequence of actions without intermediate replanning) is a key technique to improve robotic manipulation abilities. However, a large chunk size reduces the model's responsiveness to new information, while a small one increases the likelihood of mode-jumping, jerky behavior resulting from discontinuities between chunks. Therefore, selecting the optimal chunk size is an urgent demand to balance the model's reactivity and consistency. Unfortunately, a dominant trend in current VLA models is an empirical fixed chunk length at inference-time, hindering their superiority and scalability across diverse manipulation tasks. To address this issue, we propose a novel Adaptive Action Chunking (AAC) strategy, which exploits action entropy as the cue to adaptively determine the chunk size based on current predictions. Extensive experiments on a wide range of simulated and real-world robotic manipulation tasks have demonstrated that our approach substantially improves performance over the state-of-the-art alternatives. The videos and source code are publicly available at https://lance-lot.github.io/adaptive-chunking.github.io/.

</details>


### [328] [Primitive-based Truncated Diffusion for Efficient Trajectory Generation of Differential Drive Mobile Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04166)
*Long Xu,Choilam Wong,Yuhang Zhong,Junxiao Lin,Jialiang Hou,Fei Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Learning-enhanced motion planner for differential drive mobile manipulators using keypoint sequence extraction and primitive-based truncated diffusion model for improved efficiency, success rate, and optimality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve motion planning for differential drive mobile manipulators by addressing challenges in efficiency, success rate, and optimality, particularly in cluttered 3D environments where traditional methods may struggle.

Method: 1) Keypoint sequence extraction module that maps boundary states to 3D space via differentiable forward kinematics; 2) Separate encoding of point clouds and keypoints with attention-based fusion; 3) Primitive-based truncated diffusion model that samples from biased distribution for improved efficiency and diversity; 4) Trajectory optimization refinement for dynamic feasibility and task-specific optimality.

Result: In cluttered 3D simulations, the method achieves higher success rate, improved trajectory diversity, and competitive runtime compared to vanilla diffusion models and classical baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed learning-enhanced motion planner effectively combines keypoint-based task representation with primitive-based truncated diffusion to achieve superior performance in motion planning for differential drive mobile manipulators in complex 3D environments.

Abstract: We present a learning-enhanced motion planner for differential drive mobile manipulators to improve efficiency, success rate, and optimality. For task representation encoder, we propose a keypoint sequence extraction module that maps boundary states to 3D space via differentiable forward kinematics. Point clouds and keypoints are encoded separately and fused with attention, enabling effective integration of environment and boundary states information. We also propose a primitive-based truncated diffusion model that samples from a biased distribution. Compared with vanilla diffusion model, this framework improves the efficiency and diversity of the solution. Denoised paths are refined by trajectory optimization to ensure dynamic feasibility and task-specific optimality. In cluttered 3D simulations, our method achieves higher success rate, improved trajectory diversity, and competitive runtime compared to vanilla diffusion and classical baselines. The source code is released at https://github.com/nmoma/nmoma .

</details>


### [329] [Robots Need Some Education: On the complexity of learning in evolutionary robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04196)
*Fuda van Diggelen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This thesis explores the integration of Robot Learning with Evolutionary Robotics, developing specialized learning algorithms for evolutionary contexts and investigating their effects on the evolutionary process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to bridge Evolutionary Robotics and Robot Learning by understanding how to effectively integrate learning algorithms into evolutionary processes, as the effects of introducing learning into evolution are not well-understood and can be challenging to implement properly.

Method: The thesis investigates the intricacies of combining these fields and presents several learning algorithms specifically developed for an Evolutionary Robotics context, focusing on careful design of suitable learning approaches.

Result: The paper presents multiple learning algorithms designed for Evolutionary Robotics contexts, though specific results are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: Integrating Robot Learning with Evolutionary Robotics requires specialized approaches, and this thesis contributes to understanding these complexities by developing appropriate learning algorithms for evolutionary contexts.

Abstract: Evolutionary Robotics and Robot Learning are two fields in robotics that aim to automatically optimize robot designs. The key difference between them lies in what is being optimized and the time scale involved. Evolutionary Robotics is a field that applies evolutionary computation techniques to evolve the morphologies or controllers, or both. Robot Learning, on the other hand, involves any learning technique aimed at optimizing a robot's controller in a given morphology. In terms of time scales, evolution occurs across multiple generations, whereas learning takes place within the `lifespan' of an individual robot. Integrating Robot Learning with Evolutionary Robotics requires the careful design of suitable learning algorithms in the context of evolutionary robotics. The effects of introducing learning into the evolutionary process are not well-understood and can thus be tricky. This thesis investigates these intricacies and presents several learning algorithms developed for an Evolutionary Robotics context.

</details>


### [330] [RK-MPC: Residual Koopman Model Predictive Control for Quadruped Locomotion in Offroad Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04221)
*Sriram S. K. S. Narayanan,Umesh Vaidya*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RK-MPC is a data-driven MPC framework for quadruped locomotion that combines a nominal template model with a learned linear residual predictor in lifted coordinates, enabling real-time control at 500Hz with improved prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address model mismatch in quadruped locomotion caused by contact variability and terrain disturbances, while maintaining real-time tractability for practical hardware deployment.

Method: Augments nominal template model with compact linear residual predictor learned from data in lifted coordinates; embedded within convex quadratic-program MPC formulation with provable error bounds.

Result: Demonstrates reliable blind locomotion across contact disturbances, multiple gait schedules, and challenging terrains (grass, gravel, snow, ice) in both simulation and hardware (Unitree Go1) at 500Hz onboard.

Conclusion: RK-MPC provides a practical, hardware-validated pathway for data-driven predictive control of quadrupeds in unstructured environments, improving prediction fidelity while preserving real-time performance.

Abstract: This paper presents Residual Koopman MPC (RK-MPC), a Koopman-based, data-driven model predictive control framework for quadruped locomotion that improves prediction fidelity while preserving real-time tractability. RK-MPC augments a nominal template model with a compact linear residual predictor learned from data in lifted coordinates, enabling systematic correction of model mismatch induced by contact variability and terrain disturbances with provable bounds on multi-step prediction error. The learned residual model is embedded within a convex quadratic-program MPC formulation, yielding a receding-horizon controller that runs onboard at 500 Hz and retains the structure and constraint-handling advantages of optimization-based control. We evaluate RK-MPC in both Gazebo simulation and Unitree Go1 hardware experiments, demonstrating reliable blind locomotion across contact disturbances, multiple gait schedules, and challenging off-road terrains including grass, gravel, snow, and ice. We further compare against Koopman/EDMD baselines using alternative observable dictionaries, including monomial and $SE(3)$-structured bases, and show that the residual correction improves multi-step prediction and closed-loop performance while reducing sensitivity to the choice of observables. Overall, RK-MPC provides a practical, hardware-validated pathway for data-driven predictive control of quadrupeds in unstructured environments. See https://sriram-2502.github.io/rk-mpc for implementation videos.

</details>


### [331] [Precise Robot Command Understanding Using Grammar-Constrained Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04233)
*Xinyun Huo,Raghav Gnanasambandam,Xinyao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A grammar-constrained LLM that combines conversational flexibility with deterministic precision for industrial human-robot collaboration, using a two-stage process with validation feedback loop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs lack domain-specific rigidity needed for safe and executable industrial commands in human-robot collaboration, requiring a solution that balances conversational flexibility with deterministic precision.

Method: Two-stage hybrid approach: 1) Fine-tuned LLM for contextual reasoning and parameter inference, 2) Structured Language Model and grammar-based canonicalizer to constrain outputs into standardized symbolic format. Includes validation feedback loop with grammar parser for self-correction.

Result: Superior command validity compared to fine-tuned API-based LLM and standalone grammar-driven NLU model on HuRIC dataset, promoting safer and more effective industrial human-robot collaboration.

Conclusion: The grammar-constrained hybrid model successfully bridges the gap between LLM flexibility and industrial safety requirements, enabling reliable communication for human-robot collaboration through deterministic command generation with self-correction capabilities.

Abstract: Human-robot collaboration in industrial settings requires precise and reliable communication to enhance operational efficiency. While Large Language Models (LLMs) understand general language, they often lack the domain-specific rigidity needed for safe and executable industrial commands. To address this gap, this paper introduces a novel grammar-constrained LLM that integrates a grammar-driven Natural Language Understanding (NLU) system with a fine-tuned LLM, which enables both conversational flexibility and the deterministic precision required in robotics. Our method employs a two-stage process. First, a fine-tuned LLM performs high-level contextual reasoning and parameter inference on natural language inputs. Second, a Structured Language Model (SLM) and a grammar-based canonicalizer constrain the LLM's output, forcing it into a standardized symbolic format composed of valid action frames and command elements. This process guarantees that generated commands are valid and structured in a robot-readable JSON format. A key feature of the proposed model is a validation and feedback loop. A grammar parser validates the output against a predefined list of executable robotic actions. If a command is invalid, the system automatically generates corrective prompts and re-engages the LLM. This iterative self-correction mechanism allows the model to recover from initial interpretation errors to improve system robustness. We evaluate our grammar-constrained hybrid model against two baselines: a fine-tuned API-based LLM and a standalone grammar-driven NLU model. Using the Human Robot Interaction Corpus (HuRIC) dataset, we demonstrate that the hybrid approach achieves superior command validity, which promotes safer and more effective industrial human-robot collaboration.

</details>


### [332] [Real-Time Projected Adaptive Control for Closed-Chain Co-Manipulative Continuum Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04286)
*Rana Danesh,Farrokh Janabi-Sharifi,Farhad Aghili*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Projected adaptive control framework for co-manipulative continuum robots (CCRs) handling unknown flexible object parameters and closed-chain interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CCRs form closed-chain deformable systems with strong dynamic interactions and internal forces. Flexible object parameters are often unknown and vary between operations, making model-based controllers inadequate.

Method: Uses Geometric Variable Strain (GVS) representation for finite-dimensional modeling, Pfaffian velocity constraints for closed-chain interactions, orthogonal projection for constraint-consistent dynamics, and adaptive control law for online compensation of uncertain parameters.

Result: Lyapunov analysis proves closed-loop stability and convergence of task-space tracking errors to zero. Simulation and experiments on tendon-driven CCR platform validate the framework for regulation and trajectory tracking.

Conclusion: The projected adaptive control framework successfully addresses the challenges of CCRs with unknown flexible object parameters, providing stable and accurate task-space control.

Abstract: In co-manipulative continuum robots (CCRs), multiple continuum arms cooperate by grasping a common flexible object, forming a closed-chain deformable mechanical system. The closed-chain coupling induces strong dynamic interactions and internal reaction forces. Moreover, in practical tasks, the flexible object's physical parameters are often unknown and vary between operations, rendering nominal model-based controllers inadequate. This paper presents a projected adaptive control framework for CCRs formulated at the dynamic level. The coupled dynamics are expressed using the Geometric Variable Strain (GVS) representation, yielding a finite-dimensional model that accurately represents the system, preserves the linear-in-parameters structure required for adaptive control, and is suitable for real-time implementation. Closed-chain interactions are enforced through Pfaffian velocity constraints, and an orthogonal projection is used to express the dynamics in the constraint-consistent motion subspace. Based on the projected dynamics, an adaptive control law is developed to compensate online for uncertain dynamic parameters of both the continuum robots and the manipulated flexible object. Lyapunov analysis establishes closed-loop stability and convergence of the task-space tracking errors to zero. Simulation and experiments on a tendon-driven CCR platform validate the proposed framework in task-space regulation and trajectory tracking.

</details>


### [333] [frax: Fast Robot Kinematics and Dynamics in JAX](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04310)
*Daniel Morton,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: frax is a JAX-based robot dynamics library that provides high-performance, unified CPU/GPU/TPU support with automatic differentiation, achieving microsecond latencies on CPU and massive parallelization on GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robot dynamics libraries are either optimized for low-latency CPU execution or high-throughput GPU workloads, but lack a unified framework that maintains performance across architectures while being easy to use. There's a need for a library that works well on CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs without compromising performance or usability.

Method: frax is built on JAX and uses a fully-vectorized approach to robot kinematics and dynamics. It provides a pure-Python interface that automatically supports CPU, GPU, and TPU execution. The library leverages JAX's automatic differentiation capabilities for optimization-based methods and enables efficient parallelization through vectorization.

Result: On CPU: Achieves low-microsecond computation times suitable for kilohertz control rates, outperforming common Python libraries and approaching optimized C++ implementations. On GPU: Scales to thousands of instances, reaching over 100 million dynamics evaluations per second. Validated on Franka Panda manipulator and Unitree G1 humanoid.

Conclusion: frax successfully addresses the need for a high-performance, easy-to-use robot dynamics library that works across CPU, GPU, and TPU architectures without performance compromises. It enables both real-time control on CPU and massive parallelization on GPU using the same codebase, making it valuable for robot control, planning, and learning applications.

Abstract: In robot control, planning, and learning, there is a need for rigid-body dynamics libraries that are highly performant, easy to use, and compatible with CPUs and accelerators. While existing libraries often excel at either low-latency CPU execution or high-throughput GPU workloads, few provide a unified framework that targets multiple architectures without compromising performance or ease-of-use. To address this, we introduce frax, a JAX-based library for robot kinematics and dynamics, providing a high-performance, pure-Python interface across CPU, GPU, and TPU. Via a fully-vectorized approach to robot dynamics, frax enables efficient real-time control and parallelization, while supporting automatic differentiation for optimization-based methods. On CPU, frax achieves low-microsecond computation times suitable for kilohertz control rates, outperforming common libraries in Python and approaching optimized C++ implementations. On GPU, the same code scales to thousands of instances, reaching upwards of 100 million dynamics evaluations per second. We validate performance on a Franka Panda manipulator and a Unitree G1 humanoid, and release frax as an open-source library.

</details>


### [334] [Adversarial Robustness Analysis of Cloud-Assisted Autonomous Driving Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04349)
*Maher Al Islam,Amr S. El-Wakeel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a hardware-in-the-loop testbed to evaluate cross-layer vulnerabilities in cloud-assisted autonomous driving, showing how adversarial attacks on perception models and network impairments jointly undermine safety-critical autonomy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloud-assisted autonomous vehicles face cross-layer security threats where adversarial manipulation of perception models and network impairments in the vehicle-cloud link can jointly compromise safety-critical autonomy, necessitating evaluation of these vulnerabilities.

Method: Developed a hardware-in-the-loop IoV testbed integrating real-time perception, control, and communication. Used YOLOv8-based object detector on cloud, subjected to whitebox adversarial attacks (FGSM and PGD) while network adversaries induced delay and packet loss in vehicle-cloud loop.

Result: Adversarial perturbations significantly degraded perception: PGD reduced detection precision from 0.73 to 0.22 and recall from 0.68 to 0.15 at epsilon=0.04. Network delays of 150-250 ms (3-4 frame loss) and packet loss rates of 0.5-5% destabilized closed-loop control, causing delayed actuation and rule violations.

Conclusion: The findings demonstrate critical cross-layer vulnerabilities in cloud-assisted autonomous driving systems and highlight the urgent need for cross-layer resilience mechanisms to ensure safety and reliability.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles increasingly rely on deep learning-based perception and control, which impose substantial computational demands. Cloud-assisted architectures offload these functions to remote servers, enabling enhanced perception and coordinated decision-making through the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). However, this paradigm introduces cross-layer vulnerabilities, where adversarial manipulation of perception models and network impairments in the vehicle-cloud link can jointly undermine safety-critical autonomy. This paper presents a hardware-in-the-loop IoV testbed that integrates real-time perception, control, and communication to evaluate such vulnerabilities in cloud-assisted autonomous driving. A YOLOv8-based object detector deployed on the cloud is subjected to whitebox adversarial attacks using the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD), while network adversaries induce delay and packet loss in the vehicle-cloud loop. Results show that adversarial perturbations significantly degrade perception performance, with PGD reducing detection precision and recall from 0.73 and 0.68 in the clean baseline to 0.22 and 0.15 at epsilon= 0.04. Network delays of 150-250 ms, corresponding to transient losses of approximately 3-4 frames, and packet loss rates of 0.5-5 % further destabilize closed-loop control, leading to delayed actuation and rule violations. These findings highlight the need for cross-layer resilience in cloud-assisted autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [335] [Towards Considerate Human-Robot Coexistence: A Dual-Space Framework of Robot Design and Human Perception in Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04374)
*Yuanchen Bai,Zijian Ding,Ruixiang Han,Niti Parikh,Wendy Ju,Angelique Taylor*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper examines how human perceptions of robots evolve over time in real-world settings, proposing a "considerate human-robot coexistence" framework where humans actively shape robot integration as interpreters and mediators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current research focuses on static snapshots of human attitudes toward robots, lacking insight into how perceptions form and evolve dynamically as humans and robots coexist in real-world settings with organizational embeddedness, temporal evolution, and social situatedness.

Method: Conducted in-depth follow-up interviews with nine participants from a 14-week co-design study on healthcare robots to understand how perceptions evolve over time in real-world deployment.

Result: Identified a human perception space with four interpretive dimensions (degree of decomposition, temporal orientation, scope of reasoning, source of evidence) and conceptualized a co-evolving loop between human perception space and robot design space.

Conclusion: Proposes "considerate human-robot coexistence" where humans act as interpreters and mediators who actively shape how robots are understood and integrated across deployment stages, moving beyond static acceptance models to dynamic, mutually-shaping relationships.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of robotics, spanning expanded capabilities, more intuitive interaction, and more integration into real-world workflows, is reshaping what it means for humans and robots to coexist. Beyond sharing physical space, this coexistence is increasingly characterized by organizational embeddedness, temporal evolution, social situatedness, and open-ended uncertainty. However, prior work has largely focused on static snapshots of attitudes and acceptance, offering limited insight into how perceptions form and evolve, and what active role humans play in shaping coexistence as a dynamic process. We address these gaps through in-depth follow-up interviews with nine participants from a 14-week co-design study on healthcare robots. We identify the human perception space, including four interpretive dimensions (i.e., degree of decomposition, temporal orientation, scope of reasoning, and source of evidence). We enrich the conceptual framework of human-robot coexistence by conceptualizing the mutual relationship between the human perception space and the robot design space as a co-evolving loop, in which human needs, design decisions, situated interpretations, and social mediation continuously reshape one another over time. Building on this, we propose considerate human-robot coexistence, arguing that humans act not only as design contributors but also as interpreters and mediators who actively shape how robots are understood and integrated across deployment stages.

</details>


### [336] [ReinVBC: A Model-based Reinforcement Learning Approach to Vehicle Braking Controller](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04401)
*Haoxin Lin,Junjie Zhou,Daheng Xu,Yang Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReinVBC applies offline model-based reinforcement learning to vehicle braking control, reducing manual calibration needs while maintaining performance comparable to production-grade ABS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vehicle braking systems require extensive manual calibration during production, which is labor-intensive and time-consuming. The vehicle industry needs solutions that reduce this manual effort while maintaining braking controller performance and safety.

Method: ReinVBC uses offline model-based reinforcement learning with engineering designs for model learning and utilization. It creates a data-driven vehicle dynamics model and learns braking policies through exploration within this model, incorporating practical engineering considerations.

Result: The method demonstrates capability in real-world vehicle braking scenarios and shows potential to replace production-grade anti-lock braking systems (ABS).

Conclusion: Offline model-based reinforcement learning with appropriate engineering designs offers a promising solution for vehicle braking control that can reduce manual calibration requirements while maintaining safety and performance standards.

Abstract: Braking system, the key module to ensure the safety and steer-ability of current vehicles, relies on extensive manual calibration during production. Reducing labor and time consumption while maintaining the Vehicle Braking Controller (VBC) performance greatly benefits the vehicle industry. Model-based methods in offline reinforcement learning, which facilitate policy exploration within a data-driven dynamics model, offer a promising solution for addressing real-world control tasks. This work proposes ReinVBC, which applies an offline model-based reinforcement learning approach to deal with the vehicle braking control problem. We introduce useful engineering designs into the paradigm of model learning and utilization to obtain a reliable vehicle dynamics model and a capable braking policy. Several results demonstrate the capability of our method in real-world vehicle braking and its potential to replace the production-grade anti-lock braking system.

</details>


### [337] [FORMULA: FORmation MPC with neUral barrier Learning for safety Assurance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04409)
*Qintong Xie,Weishu Zhan,Peter Chin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FORMULA: A safe distributed learning-enhanced predictive control framework for multi-robot formation control that combines MPC with neural CBFs for scalable safety in complex environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MPC approaches for multi-robot systems lack scalability and provable safety guarantees, while control barrier functions are difficult to handcraft for large-scale nonlinear systems. There's a need for robust, safety-aware formation control in cluttered, dynamic environments for applications like disaster response and warehouse logistics.

Method: FORMULA integrates Model Predictive Control (MPC) with Control Lyapunov Functions (CLFs) for stability and neural network-based Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) for decentralized safety. This eliminates manual safety constraint design and enables distributed control.

Result: The framework maintains formation integrity during obstacle avoidance, resolves deadlocks in dense configurations, reduces online computational load, and enables scalable, safety-aware, formation-preserving navigation for multi-robot teams in complex environments.

Conclusion: FORMULA provides a practical solution for safe distributed formation control in multi-robot systems, addressing scalability and safety challenges through learning-enhanced predictive control with neural CBFs.

Abstract: Multi-robot systems (MRS) are essential for large-scale applications such as disaster response, material transport, and warehouse logistics, yet ensuring robust, safety-aware formation control in cluttered and dynamic environments remains a major challenge. Existing model predictive control (MPC) approaches suffer from limitations in scalability and provable safety, while control barrier functions (CBFs), though principled for safety enforcement, are difficult to handcraft for large-scale nonlinear systems. This paper presents FORMULA, a safe distributed, learning-enhanced predictive control framework that integrates MPC with Control Lyapunov Functions (CLFs) for stability and neural network-based CBFs for decentralized safety, eliminating manual safety constraint design. This scheme maintains formation integrity during obstacle avoidance, resolves deadlocks in dense configurations, and reduces online computational load. Simulation results demonstrate that FORMULA enables scalable, safety-aware, formation-preserving navigation for multi-robot teams in complex environments.

</details>


### [338] [Veo-Act: How Far Can Frontier Video Models Advance Generalizable Robot Manipulation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04502)
*Zhongru Zhang,Chenghan Yang,Qingzhou Lu,Yanjiang Guo,Jianke Zhang,Yucheng Hu,Jianyu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Video generation models like Veo-3 can support robotic manipulation through a hierarchical approach combining video prediction with inverse dynamics models and vision-language-action policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how advanced video generation models can support generalizable robotic manipulation, leveraging their understanding of physical dynamics without requiring human supervision or expert demonstrations.

Method: Two approaches: 1) Zero-shot Veo-3+IDM where Veo-3 predicts future image sequences and an inverse dynamics model recovers actions from random-play data; 2) Hierarchical Veo-Act framework using Veo-3 as high-level motion planner and VLA policy as low-level executor.

Result: Veo-3+IDM generates approximately correct task-level trajectories but lacks low-level control accuracy. Veo-Act significantly improves instruction-following performance of state-of-the-art VLA policies.

Conclusion: Video generation models can be valuable components for generalizable robot learning, especially when used hierarchically with specialized low-level controllers.

Abstract: Video generation models have advanced rapidly and are beginning to show a strong understanding of physical dynamics. In this paper, we investigate how far an advanced video generation model such as Veo-3 can support generalizable robotic manipulation. We first study a zero-shot approach in which Veo-3 predicts future image sequences from current robot observations, while an inverse dynamics model IDM recovers the corresponding robot actions. The IDM is trained solely on random-play data, requiring neither human supervision nor expert demonstrations. The key intuition is that, if a video model can generate physically plausible future motions in image space, an IDM can translate those visual trajectories into executable robot actions. We evaluate this "Veo-3+IDM" approach in both simulation and the real world using a high-dimensional dexterous hand. We find that, owing to the strong generalization capability of frontier video models, Veo-3+IDM can consistently generate approximately correct task-level trajectories. However, its low-level control accuracy remains insufficient to solve most tasks reliably. Motivated by this observation, we develop a hierarchical framework, Veo-Act, which uses Veo-3 as a high-level motion planner and a VLA policy as the low-level executor, significantly improving the instruction-following performance of a state-of-the-art vision-language-action policy. Overall, our results suggest that, as video generation models continue to improve, video models can be a valuable component for generalizable robot learning.

</details>


### [339] [G-EDF-Loc: 3D Continuous Gaussian Distance Field for Robust Gradient-Based 6DoF Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04525)
*José E. Maese,Lucía Coto-Elena,Luis Merino,Fernando Caballero*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robust 6-DoF localization system using continuous 3D distance fields with G-EDF representation for real-time scan-to-map registration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for robust 6-DoF localization that works reliably even with degraded odometry or without IMU priors, requiring efficient continuous map representations.

Method: Direct CPU-based scan-to-map registration using G-EDF (Block-Sparse Gaussian Mixture Model) with adaptive spatial partitioning for continuous distance fields and analytical gradients.

Result: Competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods on large-scale datasets, with exceptional resilience under severe odometry degradation or without IMU priors.

Conclusion: G-EDF-Loc provides robust real-time 6-DoF localization through continuous distance field representation, offering practical advantages for challenging scenarios.

Abstract: This paper presents a robust 6-DoF localization framework based on a direct, CPU-based scan-to-map registration pipeline. The system leverages G-EDF, a novel continuous and memory-efficient 3D distance field representation. The approach models the Euclidean Distance Field (EDF) using a Block-Sparse Gaussian Mixture Model with adaptive spatial partitioning, ensuring $C^1$ continuity across block transitions and mitigating boundary artifacts. By leveraging the analytical gradients of this continuous map, which maintain Eikonal consistency, the proposed method achieves high-fidelity spatial reconstruction and real-time localization. Experimental results on large-scale datasets demonstrate that G-EDF-Loc performs competitively against state-of-the-art methods, exhibiting exceptional resilience even under severe odometry degradation or in the complete absence of IMU priors.

</details>


### [340] [Visual Prompt Based Reasoning for Offroad Mapping using Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04564)
*Abdelmoamen Nasser,Yousef Baba'a,Murad Mebrahtu,Nadya Abdel Madjid,Jorge Dias,Majid Khonji*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Zero-shot off-road navigation using SAM2 for segmentation and VLM for drivability reasoning, eliminating need for separate terrain models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional off-road autonomy requires multiple specialized models (terrain classification, height estimation, slip/slope quantification) that need separate training, task-specific datasets, and fine-tuning, creating complexity and maintenance overhead.

Method: 1) Use SAM2 for zero-shot environment segmentation; 2) Annotate segmented masks with numeric labels; 3) Pass both original and segmented images to a Vision-Language Model (VLM); 4) Prompt VLM to identify drivable regions by numeric labels; 5) Combine with planning/control modules.

Result: Surpasses state-of-the-art trainable models on high-resolution segmentation datasets and enables full-stack navigation in Isaac Sim off-road environment.

Conclusion: Proposed unified framework eliminates explicit terrain-specific models by leveraging VLM's inherent reasoning capabilities, offering a simpler, more effective approach to off-road autonomy.

Abstract: Traditional approaches to off-road autonomy rely on separate models for terrain classification, height estimation, and quantifying slip or slope conditions. Utilizing several models requires training each component separately, having task specific datasets, and fine-tuning. In this work, we present a zero-shot approach leveraging SAM2 for environment segmentation and a vision-language model (VLM) to reason about drivable areas. Our approach involves passing to the VLM both the original image and the segmented image annotated with numeric labels for each mask. The VLM is then prompted to identify which regions, represented by these numeric labels, are drivable. Combined with planning and control modules, this unified framework eliminates the need for explicit terrain-specific models and relies instead on the inherent reasoning capabilities of the VLM. Our approach surpasses state-of-the-art trainable models on high resolution segmentation datasets and enables full stack navigation in our Isaac Sim offroad environment.

</details>


### [341] [Biologically Inspired Event-Based Perception and Sample-Efficient Learning for High-Speed Table Tennis Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04618)
*Ziqi Wang,Jingyue Zhao,Xun Xiao,Jichao Yang,Yaohua Wang,Shi Xu,Lei Wang,Huadong Dai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Event-based vision + sample-efficient learning for high-speed table tennis robots, improving accuracy by 35.8% over conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robots struggle with perception and decision-making in high-speed dynamic scenarios like table tennis. Frame-based vision suffers from motion blur, latency, and data redundancy, while existing event-based methods are limited to simplified scenarios. Decision-making approaches require excessive training interactions.

Method: Combines event-based perception with sample-efficient learning: 1) Event-based ball detection using motion cues and geometric consistency on raw event streams, 2) Human-inspired training strategy with progressive skill acquisition from low to high-speed scenarios using case-dependent adaptive rewards and reward-threshold mechanisms.

Result: Achieves 35.8% improvement in return-to-target accuracy with the same training episodes compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: Biologically inspired perception and decision-making approaches are effective for high-speed robotic systems, addressing limitations of conventional methods through event-based sensing and sample-efficient learning strategies.

Abstract: Perception and decision-making in high-speed dynamic scenarios remain challenging for current robots. In contrast, humans and animals can rapidly perceive and make decisions in such environments. Taking table tennis as a typical example, conventional frame-based vision sensors suffer from motion blur, high latency and data redundancy, which can hardly meet real-time, accurate perception requirements. Inspired by the human visual system, event-based perception methods address these limitations through asynchronous sensing, high temporal resolution, and inherently sparse data representations. However, current event-based methods are still restricted to simplified, unrealistic ball-only scenarios. Meanwhile, existing decision-making approaches typically require thousands of interactions with the environment to converge, resulting in significant computational costs. In this work, we present a biologically inspired approach for high-speed table tennis robots, combining event-based perception with sample-efficient learning. On the perception side, we propose an event-based ball detection method that leverages motion cues and geometric consistency, operating directly on asynchronous event streams without frame reconstruction, to achieve robust and efficient detection in real-world rallies. On the decision-making side, we introduce a human-inspired, sample-efficient training strategy that first trains policies in low-speed scenarios, progressively acquiring skills from basic to advanced, and then adapts them to high-speed scenarios, guided by a case-dependent temporally adaptive reward and a reward-threshold mechanism. With the same training episodes, our method improves return-to-target accuracy by 35.8%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of biologically inspired perception and decision-making for high-speed robotic systems.

</details>


### [342] [WaterSplat-SLAM: Photorealistic Monocular SLAM in Underwater Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04642)
*Kangxu Wang,Shaofeng Zou,Chenxing Jiang,Yixiang Dai,Siang Chen,Shaojie Shen,Guijin Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: WaterSplat-SLAM is a monocular underwater SLAM system that achieves robust pose estimation and photorealistic dense mapping by incorporating semantic medium filtering and adaptive Gaussian map management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing underwater SLAM methods struggle to produce high-fidelity rendered maps, which is crucial for applications like autonomous underwater vehicles and marine archaeology. Underwater environments present unique challenges for monocular SLAM systems.

Method: The system couples semantic medium filtering into two-view 3D reconstruction prior for underwater-adapted camera tracking and depth estimation. It also uses semantic-guided rendering and adaptive map management with an online medium-aware Gaussian map to model underwater environments photorealistically and compactly.

Result: Experiments on multiple underwater datasets demonstrate that WaterSplat-SLAM achieves robust camera tracking and high-fidelity rendering in underwater environments.

Conclusion: WaterSplat-SLAM successfully addresses the challenges of underwater monocular SLAM by integrating semantic understanding of the underwater medium with advanced mapping techniques, enabling both accurate pose estimation and photorealistic dense reconstruction.

Abstract: Underwater monocular SLAM is a challenging problem with applications from autonomous underwater vehicles to marine archaeology. However, existing underwater SLAM methods struggle to produce maps with high-fidelity rendering. In this paper, we propose WaterSplat-SLAM, a novel monocular underwater SLAM system that achieves robust pose estimation and photorealistic dense mapping. Specifically, we couple semantic medium filtering into two-view 3D reconstruction prior to enable underwater-adapted camera tracking and depth estimation. Furthermore, we present a semantic-guided rendering and adaptive map management strategy with an online medium-aware Gaussian map, modeling underwater environment in a photorealistic and compact manner. Experiments on multiple underwater datasets demonstrate that WaterSplat-SLAM achieves robust camera tracking and high-fidelity rendering in underwater environments.

</details>


### [343] [ROSClaw: A Hierarchical Semantic-Physical Framework for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04664)
*Rongfeng Zhao,Xuanhao Zhang,Zhaochen Guo,Xiang Shao,Zhongpan Zhu,Bin He,Jie Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ROSClaw is a unified agent framework that integrates policy learning and task execution within a single VLM controller for heterogeneous robots, enabling sim-to-real transfer and autonomous closed-loop skill improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA/VLN systems struggle with long-horizon sequential tasks, and modular pipelines for data collection, skill training, and policy deployment are costly and inefficient. There's a critical gap between semantic understanding and physical execution in embodied AI.

Method: Uses e-URDF representations of heterogeneous robots as physical constraints to create sim-to-real topological mapping. Implements data collection/state accumulation mechanism storing robot states, multimodal observations, and execution trajectories. Features unified VLM controller maintaining semantic continuity and dynamically assigning task-specific control to different agents.

Result: Enables real-time access to physical states of simulated/real agents, supports hardware-level validation, automated SDK-level control program generation, tool-based execution, rapid cross-platform transfer, and continual robotic skill improvement.

Conclusion: ROSClaw establishes an autonomous closed-loop framework that minimizes reliance on robot-specific development workflows, improving robustness in multi-policy execution and bridging the gap between semantic reasoning and physical execution.

Abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) with embodied agents has improved high-level reasoning capabilities; however, a critical gap remains between semantic understanding and physical execution. While vision-language-action (VLA) and vision-language-navigation (VLN) systems enable robots to perform manipulation and navigation tasks from natural language instructions, they still struggle with long-horizon sequential and temporally structured tasks. Existing frameworks typically adopt modular pipelines for data collection, skill training, and policy deployment, resulting in high costs in experimental validation and policy optimization. To address these limitations, we propose ROSClaw, an agent framework for heterogeneous robots that integrates policy learning and task execution within a unified vision-language model (VLM) controller. The framework leverages e-URDF representations of heterogeneous robots as physical constraints to construct a sim-to-real topological mapping, enabling real-time access to the physical states of both simulated and real-world agents. We further incorporate a data collection and state accumulation mechanism that stores robot states, multimodal observations, and execution trajectories during real-world execution, enabling subsequent iterative policy optimization. During deployment, a unified agent maintains semantic continuity between reasoning and execution, and dynamically assigns task-specific control to different agents, thereby improving robustness in multi-policy execution. By establishing an autonomous closed-loop framework, ROSClaw minimizes the reliance on robot-specific development workflows. The framework supports hardware-level validation, automated generation of SDK-level control programs, and tool-based execution, enabling rapid cross-platform transfer and continual improvement of robotic skills. Ours project page: https://www.rosclaw.io/.

</details>


### [344] [Pickalo: Leveraging 6D Pose Estimation for Low-Cost Industrial Bin Picking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04690)
*Alessandro Tarsi,Matteo Mastrogiuseppe,Saverio Taliani,Simone Cortinovis,Ugo Pattacini*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pickalo is a low-cost 6D pose-based bin-picking system using wrist-mounted RGB-D camera with active scene exploration, BridgeDepth for refined depth, synthetic-trained Mask-RCNN for segmentation, zero-shot SAM-6D for pose estimation, pose buffer for multi-view fusion, and utility-based grasp planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial bin picking faces challenges with severe clutter, occlusions, and high costs of traditional 3D sensing setups. There's a need for robust, cost-effective solutions that can handle realistic industrial conditions.

Method: Modular pipeline using low-cost hardware: wrist-mounted RGB-D camera for active multi-view exploration, BridgeDepth for refined depth estimation, Mask-RCNN trained on synthetic data for segmentation, zero-shot SAM-6D for pose estimation, pose buffer for multi-view fusion and symmetry handling, offline grasp candidate generation, and online utility-based grasp planning with collision checking.

Result: Achieves up to 600 mean picks per hour with 96-99% grasp success rate, robust performance over 30-minute runs on densely filled euroboxes. Ablation studies show benefits of enhanced depth estimation and pose buffer for long-term stability and throughput.

Conclusion: Pickalo demonstrates that low-cost hardware combined with advanced computer vision and planning algorithms can achieve high-performance bin picking in realistic industrial conditions, addressing challenges of clutter, occlusions, and cost.

Abstract: Bin picking in real industrial environments remains challenging due to severe clutter, occlusions, and the high cost of traditional 3D sensing setups. We present Pickalo, a modular 6D pose-based bin-picking pipeline built entirely on low-cost hardware. A wrist-mounted RGB-D camera actively explores the scene from multiple viewpoints, while raw stereo streams are processed with BridgeDepth to obtain refined depth maps suitable for accurate collision reasoning. Object instances are segmented with a Mask-RCNN model trained purely on photorealistic synthetic data and localized using the zero-shot SAM-6D pose estimator. A pose buffer module fuses multi-view observations over time, handling object symmetries and significantly reducing pose noise. Offline, we generate and curate large sets of antipodal grasp candidates per object; online, a utility-based ranking and fast collision checking are queried for the grasp planning. Deployed on a UR5e with a parallel-jaw gripper and an Intel RealSense D435i, Pickalo achieves up to 600 mean picks per hour with 96-99% grasp success and robust performance over 30-minute runs on densely filled euroboxes. Ablation studies demonstrate the benefits of enhanced depth estimation and of the pose buffer for long-term stability and throughput in realistic industrial conditions. Videos are available at https://mesh-iit.github.io/project-jl2-camozzi/

</details>


### [345] [AnyUser: Translating Sketched User Intent into Domestic Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04811)
*Songyuan Yang,Huibin Tan,Kailun Yang,Wenjing Yang,Shaowu Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AnyUser is a unified robotic instruction system that interprets free-form sketches on camera images (optionally with language) to generate executable robot actions without requiring prior maps or models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between advanced robotic capabilities and accessible non-expert interaction, enabling intuitive domestic task instruction for diverse users including elderly, non-verbal, and low technical literacy individuals.

Method: Multimodal fusion of sketch, vision, and language inputs interpreted as spatial-semantic primitives, combined with a hierarchical policy for robust action generation. No prior maps or models required.

Result: High accuracy on sketch-based command interpretation benchmarks; successful real-world validation on two robotic platforms (KUKA LBR iiwa and Realman RMC-AIDAL) for tasks like targeted wiping and area cleaning; user study showing 85.7%-96.4% task completion rates and high user satisfaction.

Conclusion: AnyUser provides an effective foundation for practical assistive robots adaptable to real-world human environments by enabling intuitive multimodal instruction without requiring technical expertise from users.

Abstract: We introduce AnyUser, a unified robotic instruction system for intuitive domestic task instruction via free-form sketches on camera images, optionally with language. AnyUser interprets multimodal inputs (sketch, vision, language) as spatial-semantic primitives to generate executable robot actions requiring no prior maps or models. Novel components include multimodal fusion for understanding and a hierarchical policy for robust action generation. Efficacy is shown via extensive evaluations: (1) Quantitative benchmarks on the large-scale dataset showing high accuracy in interpreting diverse sketch-based commands across various simulated domestic scenes. (2) Real-world validation on two distinct robotic platforms, a statically mounted 7-DoF assistive arm (KUKA LBR iiwa) and a dual-arm mobile manipulator (Realman RMC-AIDAL), performing representative tasks like targeted wiping and area cleaning, confirming the system's ability to ground instructions and execute them reliably in physical environments. (3) A comprehensive user study involving diverse demographics (elderly, simulated non-verbal, low technical literacy) demonstrating significant improvements in usability and task specification efficiency, achieving high task completion rates (85.7%-96.4%) and user satisfaction. AnyUser bridges the gap between advanced robotic capabilities and the need for accessible non-expert interaction, laying the foundation for practical assistive robots adaptable to real-world human environments.

</details>


### [346] [Efficient Multi-Objective Planning with Weighted Maximization Using Large Neighbourhood Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04826)
*Krishna Kalavadia,Shamak Dutta,Yash Vardhan Pant,Stephen L. Smith*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Novel Large Neighbourhood Search algorithm efficiently solves weighted maximum planning for multi-objective autonomous navigation, achieving 10-100x speedup over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weighted sum scalarization for multi-objective navigation misses critical Pareto-optimal solutions, especially in non-convex trade-off spaces. Weighted maximum methods can find all solutions but are computationally expensive, limiting practical use.

Method: Proposed a novel search algorithm based on the Large Neighbourhood Search framework to efficiently solve the weighted maximum planning problem for autonomous navigation.

Result: Achieved comparable solution quality to existing weighted maximum planners with runtime improvement of 1-2 orders of magnitude (10-100x faster), making it viable for practical autonomous navigation.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithm successfully addresses the computational complexity challenge of weighted maximum planning, enabling practical use of this superior multi-objective optimization approach for autonomous navigation systems.

Abstract: Autonomous navigation often requires the simultaneous optimization of multiple objectives. The most common approach scalarizes these into a single cost function using a weighted sum, but this method is unable to find all possible trade-offs and can therefore miss critical solutions. An alternative, the weighted maximum of objectives, can find all Pareto-optimal solutions, including those in non-convex regions of the trade-off space that weighted sum methods cannot find. However, the increased computational complexity of finding weighted maximum solutions in the discrete domain has limited its practical use. To address this challenge, we propose a novel search algorithm based on the Large Neighbourhood Search framework that efficiently solves the weighted maximum planning problem. Through extensive simulations, we demonstrate that our algorithm achieves comparable solution quality to existing weighted maximum planners with a runtime improvement of 1-2 orders of magnitude, making it a viable option for autonomous navigation.

</details>


### [347] [Outlier-Robust Nonlinear Moving Horizon Estimation using Adaptive Loss Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04862)
*Nestor Deniz,Guido Sanchez,Fernando Auat Cheein,Leonardo Giovanini*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes adaptive robust loss function framework for MHE that handles outliers while maintaining good performance with clean data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional MHE methods are sensitive to outliers in measurements, which can severely degrade estimation performance. Need robust approach that can handle contaminated data while maintaining efficiency with clean data.

Method: Adaptive robust loss function framework with regularization term to avoid naive solutions. Includes tuning parameter to control loss function shape and robustness to outliers. Prioritizes fitting uncontaminated data while downweighting contaminated measurements.

Result: Simulation shows adaptation occurs in just a few iterations. Traditional L2 behavior predominates when measurements are free of outliers. Framework effectively reduces impact of outliers while maintaining good performance with clean data.

Conclusion: Proposed adaptive robust loss function provides effective outlier handling for MHE, with automatic adaptation to data quality and preservation of traditional performance with clean measurements.

Abstract: In this work, we propose an adaptive robust loss function framework for MHE, integrating an adaptive robust loss function to reduce the impact of outliers with a regularization term that avoids naive solutions. The proposed approach prioritizes the fitting of uncontaminated data and downweights the contaminated ones. A tuning parameter is incorporated into the framework to control the shape of the loss function for adjusting the estimator's robustness to outliers. The simulation results demonstrate that adaptation occurs in just a few iterations, whereas the traditional behaviour $\mathrm{L_2}$ predominates when the measurements are free of outliers.

</details>


### [348] [Copilot-Assisted Second-Thought Framework for Brain-to-Robot Hand Motion Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.27492)
*Yizhe Li,Shixiao Wang,Jian K. Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A CNN-attention hybrid model decodes hand kinematics from EEG during grasp-and-lift tasks, achieving strong within-subject performance. Extended to EEG-EMG multimodal decoding yields further improvements, with decoded trajectories used to control a robotic arm. A copilot framework filters low-confidence points to enhance trajectory fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motor kinematics prediction from EEG is crucial for developing movement-related brain-computer interfaces. While Transformers show promise for long sequential EEG data, there's a need for effective decoding methods for hand kinematics during grasp-and-lift tasks, with potential applications in robotic control.

Method: Proposed a CNN-attention hybrid model for decoding hand kinematics from EEG. Extended to EEG-EMG multimodal decoding. Introduced a copilot framework with motion-state-aware critic within a finite-state machine to filter low-confidence decoded points as post-processing.

Result: Within-subject tests achieved PCC values of 0.9854, 0.9946, and 0.9065 for X, Y, Z axes. Cross-subject tests resulted in 0.9643, 0.9795, and 0.5852. The copilot framework improved EEG-only decoding PCC to 0.93 while excluding fewer than 20% of data points. Decoded trajectories successfully controlled a Franka Panda robotic arm in simulation.

Conclusion: The CNN-attention hybrid model effectively decodes hand kinematics from EEG, with multimodal EEG-EMG decoding providing substantial improvements. The copilot framework enhances trajectory fidelity through intelligent filtering, demonstrating practical potential for brain-computer interface applications in robotic control.

Abstract: Motor kinematics prediction (MKP) from electroencephalography (EEG) is an important research area for developing movement-related brain-computer interfaces (BCIs). While traditional methods often rely on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or recurrent neural networks (RNNs), Transformer-based models have shown strong ability in modeling long sequential EEG data. In this study, we propose a CNN-attention hybrid model for decoding hand kinematics from EEG during grasp-and-lift tasks, achieving strong performance in within-subject experiments. We further extend this approach to EEG-EMG multimodal decoding, which yields substantially improved results. Within-subject tests achieve PCC values of 0.9854, 0.9946, and 0.9065 for the X, Y, and Z axes, respectively, computed on the midpoint trajectory between the thumb and index finger, while cross-subject tests result in 0.9643, 0.9795, and 0.5852. The decoded trajectories from both modalities are then used to control a Franka Panda robotic arm in a MuJoCo simulation. To enhance trajectory fidelity, we introduce a copilot framework that filters low-confidence decoded points using a motion-state-aware critic within a finite-state machine. This post-processing step improves the overall within-subject PCC of EEG-only decoding to 0.93 while excluding fewer than 20% of the data points.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [349] [Regularized Approximate Message Passing for Overloaded Discrete Linear Inversion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03717)
*Shreesal Shrestha,Getuar Rexhepi,Kuranage Roche Rayan Ranasinghe,Hyeon Seok Rou,Giuseppe Thadeu Freitas de Abreu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: RAMP is a low-complexity algorithm for overloaded MIMO detection that replaces iterative matrix inversions with adaptive scalar denoisers, achieving near-optimal performance at O(NM) complexity instead of O(M³).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current state-of-the-art IDLS for discrete signal detection in overloaded MIMO systems has prohibitive O(M³) complexity due to iterative matrix inversions, making it impractical despite near-optimal performance.

Method: RAMP extends AMP framework with adaptive, state-dependent scalar denoisers that enforce discrete constellation constraints. A robust variant adds ℓ₂-norm penalty similar to LMMSE estimator for noise resilience.

Result: Simulations show RAMP closely tracks exact IDLS performance while avoiding catastrophic AMP failure in overloaded regimes, achieving steep BER waterfall curves at dramatically reduced computational cost.

Conclusion: RAMP provides an efficient alternative to IDLS for overloaded MIMO detection, maintaining near-optimal discrete-aware performance while reducing complexity from O(M³) to O(NM) per iteration.

Abstract: We propose regularized approximate message passing (RAMP), a low-complexity algorithm for discrete signal detection in overloaded multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems where the number of transmit antennas exceeds the number of receive antennas. While the state-of-the-art (SotA) iterative discrete least squares (IDLS) framework achieves near-optimal discrete-aware performance, its iterative matrix inversions impose a prohibitive $\mathcal{O}(M^3)$ complexity. RAMP resolves this by deriving an adaptive, state-dependent scalar denoiser that enforces arbitrary discrete constellation constraints within the approximate message passing (AMP) framework, reducing per-iteration complexity to $\mathcal{O}(NM)$. A robust variant is further proposed by incorporating an $\ell_2$-norm penalty, analogous to a linear minimum mean squared error (LMMSE) estimator, to enhance noise resilience. Simulation results under uncorrelated Rayleigh fading demonstrate that both proposed algorithms closely track their exact IDLS counterparts while avoiding the catastrophic failure of standard AMP in the overloaded regime, achieving steep bit error rate (BER) waterfall curves at a fraction of the computational cost.

</details>


### [350] [Measurement driven birth model for the generalized labeled multi-Bernoulli filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03918)
*S Lin,BT Vo,SE Nordholm*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a measurement-driven birth model for GLMB filter that adaptively generates target births from measurements, eliminating need for prior knowledge of birth distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional multi-target tracking filters require prior knowledge of target birth distributions, which is often unavailable in practice. This limits their applicability in real-world scenarios where target births are unknown or unpredictable.

Method: Develops a measurement-driven birth (MDB) model for the generalized labeled multi-Bernoulli (GLMB) filter. The model adaptively generates target births based on measurement data rather than relying on predefined birth distributions.

Result: Numerical results demonstrate the performance of the proposed MDB-GLMB filter, showing it can effectively track multiple targets without requiring prior knowledge of birth distributions.

Conclusion: The measurement-driven birth model successfully eliminates the dependence on a priori knowledge of target birth distributions, making the GLMB filter more practical for real-world multi-target tracking applications.

Abstract: This paper presents a measurement driven birth (MDB) model for the generalized labeled multi-Bernoulli (GLMB) filter. The MDB model adaptively generates target births based on measurement data, thereby eliminating the dependence of \textit{a priori} knowledge of target birth distributions. Numerical results are provided to demonstrate the performance.

</details>


### [351] [Enhancing 6G Wireless Intelligence: Do LLMs Work for CSI Prediction?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04028)
*Mohsen Kazemian,Jürgen Jasperneite*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a physics-aware LLM-based framework for channel prediction in high-mobility OTFS systems, incorporating mobility descriptors to improve accuracy in rapidly time-varying environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In high-mobility 6G scenarios, rapidly time-varying channels lead to short coherence times, making conventional pilot-based CSI estimation prone to outdated information or excessive pilot overhead. Channel prediction becomes essential in such dynamic wireless systems.

Method: Develops a physics-aware LLM-based predictor that learns temporal evolution of OTFS channel coefficients from historical channel observations while incorporating mobility-related physical descriptors (e.g., maximum Doppler frequency) to achieve accurate prediction of future channel states.

Result: Extensive simulations under user velocities ranging from 100 to 500 km/h show the proposed method consistently achieves lower normalized mean square error (NMSE) compared with both classical deep learning predictors and LLM-based predictors without physical channel descriptors.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the advantage of integrating mobility-related channel knowledge with LLM-based sequence modeling for channel prediction in highly dynamic OTFS systems, showing improved performance over existing approaches.

Abstract: In high-mobility 6G scenarios, rapidly time-varying channels lead to very short coherence times, which makes conventional pilot-based channel state information (CSI) estimation approaches prone to outdated information or excessive pilot overhead. Therefore, channel prediction becomes essential in such dynamic wireless systems. To address this challenge, large language models (LLMs) are emerging learning frameworks that have recently attracted attention for CSI prediction due to their strong sequence modeling capability and ability to generalize across different environments. This paper proposes an LLM-based framework for channel prediction in high-mobility orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) communication systems. In this work, we develop a physics-aware LLM-based predictor that learns the temporal evolution of OTFS channel coefficients from historical channel observations while incorporating mobility-related physical descriptors (e.g., maximum Doppler frequency) to achieve accurate prediction of future channel states in rapidly time-varying environments. The effectiveness of the proposed framework is evaluated through extensive simulations under user velocities ranging from 100 to 500 km/h. Numerical results show that the proposed method consistently achieves lower normalized mean square error (NMSE) compared with both classical deep learning predictors and LLM-based predictors without physical channel descriptors. These results demonstrate the advantage of integrating mobility-related channel knowledge with LLM-based sequence modeling for channel prediction in highly dynamic OTFS systems.

</details>


### [352] [Unlocking the Energy-Saving Potential in O-RAN Cell-Free Massive MIMO by Joint Orchestration of Radio, Wireless Fronthaul, and Cloud Resources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.04073)
*Ozan Alp Topal,Özlem Tuğfe Demir,Emil Björnson,Cicek Cavdar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Joint orchestration of radio, fronthaul, and processing resources in O-RAN-based cell-free massive MIMO with wireless fronthaul achieves up to 70% energy savings while meeting UE rate requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To realize the energy-saving potential of cell-free massive MIMO networks by leveraging O-RAN's virtualization capabilities and eliminating fiber deployment through wireless fronthaul.

Method: Developed a joint orchestration framework with two algorithms: scenario-sampling/group-Lasso for centralized precoding and block-coordinate descent for distributed precoding, optimizing end-to-end power consumption under wireless fronthaul constraints.

Result: Centralized precoding significantly outperforms distributed precoding. Joint orchestration provides up to 70% energy savings vs cloud-only and 15% vs radio-only orchestration. Distributed antenna deployment reduces network power consumption substantially compared to concentrated deployment.

Conclusion: Cell-free massive MIMO with O-RAN and wireless fronthaul can deliver both high performance and energy efficiency, with distributed antenna deployment being crucial for power reduction in future mobile networks.

Abstract: Network virtualization and cloudification in Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN) enable joint orchestration of the processing and fronthaul resources, which are essential for realizing the energy-saving potential of cell-free massive MIMO networks. To harness this potential, we investigate cell-free massive MIMO deployed over an O-RAN architecture with a wireless fronthaul that removes the need for fiber deployment. We first model the end-to-end power consumption under wireless fronthaul.
